seed_question: What is the solution for termite (deemak/geda) infestation in wheat crop?
seed_answer: Termites (known locally as deemak, geda, or dhais) are one of the most destructive underground pests of wheat in Uttar Pradesh, particularly in districts like Basti. They attack the root system and lower stem portions, causing significant yield losses if not managed properly.   Identification and Damage Symptoms: Termites are social insects living in colonies underground. Worker termites are pale white to cream-colored, soft-bodied insects about 3-4 mm long. They attack wheat plants from sowing to maturity. Early symptoms include yellowing and drying of leaves, stunted plant growth, and withering of tillers. Affected plants can be easily pulled out as the roots are damaged. In severe cases, entire patches of the field may dry up, appearing as if affected by drought. The damage is more severe in fields with high organic matter content, undecomposed crop residues, or near trees and wooden structures.   Integrated Management Strategy:  1. Preventive Cultural Practices:  - Deep summer ploughing (May-June) to expose termite colonies and galleries to heat and predators  - Remove and destroy crop stubble, wood pieces, and organic debris that serve as termite food  - Maintain proper field sanitation and clean field boundaries  - Ensure optimal soil moisture through timely irrigation; termites thrive in dry soil conditions  - Use well-decomposed farmyard manure (FYM) instead of fresh organic matter  - Practice crop rotation with non-host crops    2. Seed Treatment:  Before sowing, treat wheat seeds with Chlorpyriphos 20% EC or thiomethoxam 30% FS @ of 3 ml per kg seed. Mix thoroughly and dry in shade before sowing. This protects early crop establishment.    3. Soil Treatment at Sowing:  Mix 1 kg per acre biopesticide Beauvaria bassiana 1.15% with 24–28 kg per acre of farm yard manure, sprinkle water on it and keep it for 8-10 days in shade and pour in the soil before sowing at the time oflast plough.r.   4. Standing Crop Treatment:  If termite infestation is observed in the standing crop:  Use 1 litre per acre of Chlorpyrifos 20% EC with irrigation water for control of termite/weevils in standing crop.   5. Biological Management:  Encourage natural enemies like birds, ants, and insect parasitoids. Soil-dwelling beneficial fungi like Beauveria bassiana and Metarhizium anisopliae can be used as bio-control agents when the termite population is low.   Safety Precautions:  - Wear protective clothing, gloves, and masks during pesticide application  - Do not eat, drink, or smoke while handling chemicals  - Wash hands and exposed body parts thoroughly after application  - Store pesticides away from food, feed, and children  - Follow recommended doses strictly; higher doses cause environmental pollution   Timing and Monitoring: Regular field monitoring is essential, especially during dry spells. Early detection and immediate action prevent severe damage. In Basti district conditions, October-November (sowing time) and February-March (when soil moisture decreases) are critical periods.   For further guidance on termite management specific to your field conditions, please contact your nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) in Basti district. KVK experts can provide soil-specific recommendations and demonstrate proper application techniques.

generated_question: My paddy plants are getting damaged, and the grains are not filling properly. What could be the problem?
generated_answer: It sounds like you might have Gundhi Bug, also called Naavaai Poochi. These bugs are greenish-brown and can be found on the rice plants. They suck the sap from the grains, making them shriveled and discolored. This happens mostly during the milky stage of the crop. If you see a lot of these bugs, you might need to spray insecticides like Malathion or Imidacloprid. You can also try using neem extract. Make sure to wear protective gear when applying chemicals. Always follow the dosage instructions carefully.
seed_question: What is the management strategy for Naavaai Poochi (Gundhi Bug) in paddy fields?
seed_answer: Naavaai Poochi, commonly known as Gundhi Bug or Rice Stink Bug (Leptocorisa acuta), is a significant pest of paddy in Tamil Nadu and other rice-growing regions. This pest primarily attacks during the milky to dough stages of grain development, causing considerable yield loss and quality deterioration.   Pest Identification and Description: The adult Gundhi Bug is a slender, greenish-brown insect measuring 15-18 mm in length with prominent antennae. The pest emits a characteristic foul odor when disturbed. Nymphs are smaller, wingless, and light green in color. Both adults and nymphs feed on developing grains by sucking sap from the milky stage panicles.   Damage Symptoms: The pest causes chaffy or partially filled grains by sucking the sap from developing rice grains. Affected grains become discolored, shriveled, and lighter in weight. The feeding activity results in reduced grain quality and market value. Heavy infestations during the milky stage can lead to 20-30% yield loss. The characteristic foul smell from the bug also affects grain quality.   Severity Assessment: Monitor fields regularly from flowering to grain formation stages. Economic threshold level is 5 bugs/ 100 ear heads at flowering and 16 bugs/100 ear heads from milky stage to grain maturity. Initial infestations show scattered chaffy grains, while severe infestations result in significant panicle damage with numerous empty or partially filled grains.   Control Measures:   For Severe Infestations (Above threshold level): Chemical control is recommended: - Malathion 50% EC at 200 ml/acre in 200–240 liters of water - Imidacloprid 06 % + Lambda-cyhalothrin 04 % SL @ 120 ml per acre in 200 liters of water - Dinotefuran 15 % + Pymetrozine 45% WG @ 135 g/acre in 200 liters of water  Apply insecticides during early morning or evening hours when bugs are active.  - ETL : 5 bugs/100 ear heads at flowering and 16 bugs/100 ear heads from milky stage to grain maturity. - KKM dust formulation consists of 10% of Acorus calamus rhizome powder and 90% of fly ash. This dust formulation repels the rice ear head bug. - Since the occurrence of the bug coincides with the flowering stage, application of the insecticide may be done either before 9 a.m. or after 3 p.m. so that fertilization of the flowers is not adversely affected.  Botanical Methods: Use any one of the following : -  Neem seed kernel extract 5%@ 4 kg/acre   For Initial Infestations:  - Cultural practices: Remove alternate host plants like grasses from field bunds  - Avoid late-planted crops which are more susceptible  - Drain water from fields temporarily to reduce pest population   Protective Gear (When Applying Chemical Insecticides): Farmers must wear chemical-resistant gloves, long-sleeved shirts, and full-length trousers. Use a face mask or respirator (N95 or equivalent) to prevent inhalation of spray droplets. Wear protective eyewear or face shield. Avoid spraying during windy conditions and ensure proper ventilation. Wash hands and exposed body parts thoroughly with soap after application.   Safety Disclaimer: Strictly follow the recommended dosages mentioned above. Do not spray during flowering or when beneficial insects are active. Store pesticides in original containers away from food items and out of children's reach. Dispose of empty containers safely by crushing and burying them. Never reuse pesticide containers for any other purpose.   For further guidance or clarification, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

Economic threshold level is 5 bugs/ 100 ear heads at flowering and 16 bugs/100 ear heads from milky stage to grain maturity. Initial infestations show scattered chaffy grains, while severe infestations result in significant panicle damage with numerous empty or partially filled grains.   Control Measures:   For Severe Infestations (Above threshold level): Chemical control is recommended: - Malathion 50% EC at 200 ml/acre in 200–240 liters of water - Imidacloprid 06 % + Lambda-cyhalothrin 04 % SL @ 120 ml per acre in 200 liters of water - Dinotefuran 15 % + Pymetrozine 45% WG @ 135 g/acre in 200 liters of water  Apply insecticides during early morning or evening hours when bugs are active.  - ETL : 5 bugs/100 ear heads at flowering and 16 bugs/100 ear heads from milky stage to grain maturity. - KKM dust formulation consists of 10% of Acorus calamus rhizome powder and 90% of fly ash. This dust formulation repels the rice ear head bug. - Since the occurrence of the bug coincides with the flowering stage, application of the insecticide may be done either before 9 a.m. or after 3 p.m. so that fertilization of the flowers is not adversely affected.  Botanical Methods: Use any one of the following : -  Neem seed kernel extract 5%@ 4 kg/acre   For Initial Infestations:  - Cultural practices: Remove alternate host plants like grasses from field bunds  - Avoid late-planted crops which are more susceptible  - Drain water from fields temporarily to reduce pest population   Protective Gear (When Applying Chemical Insecticides): Farmers must wear chemical-resistant gloves, long-sleeved shirts, and full-length trousers. Use a face mask or respirator (N95 or equivalent) to prevent inhalation of spray droplets. Wear protective eyewear or face shield. Avoid spraying during windy conditions and ensure proper ventilation. Wash hands and exposed body parts thoroughly with soap after application.   Safety Disclaimer: Strictly follow the recommended dosages mentioned above. Do not spray during flowering or when beneficial insects are active. Store pesticides in original containers away from food items and out of children's reach. Dispose of empty containers safely by crushing and burying them. Never reuse pesticide containers for any other purpose. seed_question: What is the management strategy for Naavaai Poochi (Gundhi Bug) in paddy fields?

generated_question: Is it okay to use Nirog on my wheat seeds to keep them healthy?

seed_question: Can we treat Wheat seeds with the fungicide called Nirog for disease control?
seed_answer: Yes, wheat seeds can be treated with fungicides before sowing to protect against various seed- and soil-borne diseases. However, the mentioned product is a biological fungicide (bio-fungicide), and it contains Trichoderma viride 1.5% WP (Wettable Powder). For wheat seed treatment in Uttar Pradesh, let me provide you with comprehensive guidance on proper seed treatment practices.  Importance of seed treatment:  Seed treatment is a crucial preventive measure that protects wheat crops from major diseases like loose smut (Kaniari/Kala Geri), Karnal bunt, flag smut, and various seedling blights. It also helps control seed-borne pathogens and provides early protection against soil-borne fungi. In UP conditions, where wheat is sown in November-December, seed treatment ensures healthy germination and robust seedling establishment.  Application method for seed treatment:   - For loose smut: Apply Trichoderma viride 1.50% WP at 4 grams/kg of seeds. For seed treatment: Mix the required quantity of seeds with the required quantity of Trichoderma viride 1.50% WP and ensure uniform coating, shade dry, and sow.  - For Loose Smut (Kaniari) and Common Bunt:  - Carboxin 37.5% + Thiram 37.5% WS: 2.5 grams per kg of seed  - Tebuconazole 2% DS: 1.0-1.5 grams per kg of seed  Important Note:  Before purchasing any fungicide product, please verify the active ingredient and ensure it is registered by the Central Insecticides Board (CIB). If 'Nirog' is available in your local market, check its label for active ingredients and follow the manufacturer's recommended dosage. Always purchase genuine products from authorized dealers and check for the manufacturing date and batch number.  For further guidance on seed treatment and disease management specific to your location in Kaimganj block, Farrukhabad district, please contact your nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or the District Agriculture Office. They can provide location-specific recommendations and verify the authenticity of fungicide products available in your area.

generated_question: My paddy field has a lot of wild onion, what can I do about it?
generated_answer: You can control wild onion in your paddy by using herbicides. Keep 2-3 inches of water in the field when applying the chemicals.
seed_question: How can I control wild onion (Mathia) in my Paddy crop in Punjab?
seed_answer: Wild onion (Mathia) in paddy fields can be controlled through integrated weed management practices. Pre-emergence herbicides like Pretilachlor 50% EC at 600 ml/acre or Pyrazosulfuron ethyl 10% WP at 60 gm/acre can be applied within 3 days after transplanting. For post-emergence control, use Bispyribac sodium 10% SC at 100 ml/acre at 20-25 days after transplanting. Hand weeding at 20-25 and 40-45 days after transplanting is also effective. Maintain proper water level of 2-3 inches in the field during herbicide application.

generated_question: My wheat plants are getting some kind of bug that's making the leaves look weird, what could it be?
generated_answer: It might not be whitefly. The problem could be thrips. They are small, brown or black insects that feed on the stem and sheath of the flag leaf. They can make the plant look silver. You can use blue pan traps to check for them. You can paint empty tins blue or use grease, Vaseline, or castor oil on them. Check the traps daily. If you see insects, bring a sample to the nearest KVK for help.
seed_question: How to control whitefly in wheat crop?
seed_answer: Whitefly (Bemisia tabaci) is a significant polyphagus sucking pest that can affect crops, particularly during the early growth stages. These tiny, white-winged insects feed on plant sap from the undersides of leaves, causing damage through direct feeding and by transmitting viral diseases. Understanding proper control measures is essential for maintaining healthy wheat crops and preventing yield losses.  However, this is not a pest for wheat crop. The similar damaging symptoms in wheat are caused by thrips. Adults and nymphs both are very small, brown or black insects with a tapering, segmented abdomen, elongated and fast moving measuring 2 mm in length with four narrow fringed wings.  Damaging symptoms: They are usually infecting the sheath of the flag leaf, feeding on the stem. However, leaves, stems, and heads may be attacked. Adults and nymphs both can cause damage and, if present in large numbers, may cause the tissue on which they are feeding to take on a silver coloration.  Management: - Set up blue pan traps 15 cm above the canopy for monitoring thrips @ 4-10 traps (15 X 7.5cm)/acre. Locally available empty tins can be painted blue/ coated with grease/ Vaseline/castor oil on outer surface may also be used as blue pan trap. Count the number of thrips on the traps daily and take the appropriate decision regarding management practices. - Alternatively Use yellow sticky traps for aphids and blue sticky traps for thrips @ 4-5 traps/acre.  However, if the observed symptoms or pest description do not match the pest and symptoms present in the field, the farmer is advised to install yellow sticky traps and monitor whether any insects are attracted and trapped.  If insects are found on the traps, the farmer should collect a crop sample along with the used trap containing the trapped insects and take them to the nearest KVK for proper identification. Based on accurate pest identification, appropriate and recommended control measures can then be advised.   For further guidance or clarification, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

seed_question: How to control whitefly in wheat crop?

seed_question: How to control Rust-red Flour Beetle (flower beetle) in stored wheat grains?
seed_answer: The Rust-red Flour Beetle (Tribolium castaneum), locally known as 'flour beetle' or 'laal ghun', is one of the most destructive storage pests affecting stored wheat grains in Uttar Pradesh. This pest causes significant post-harvest losses by damaging grain quality, reducing weight, and contaminating stored produce with frass and dead insects.   Detection and Identification: The adult beetle is small (3-4 mm long), reddish-brown in color, and has a flattened body. They are often found in flour, broken grains, and wheat products. Infested grains show signs of fine powdery material, webbing, and a characteristic musty odor. The larvae are yellowish-white, cylindrical, and feed actively on damaged or broken grains. Both adults and larvae cause damage, with a complete life cycle completed in 30-50 days under warm conditions (25-35°C), making multiple generations possible during the storage period.   Preventive Cultural Practices: Proper storage management is crucial for preventing rust-red flour beetle infestation. Before storage, thoroughly clean the storage facility (godown, bins, or bags) and ensure it is free from old grain residues, dust, and debris. Sun-dry the harvested wheat to reduce moisture content below 12%, as beetles thrive in moist conditions (above 13% moisture). Use sound, unbroken grains for storage, as damaged grains are more susceptible to attack. Maintain proper ventilation in storage areas and avoid overcrowding of bags. Regular inspection of stored grains (at least once every 15 days) helps in early detection of infestation.   Physical and Mechanical Control: For small quantities of grain, expose infested wheat to direct sunlight for 4-5 hours, as high temperatures (above 50°C) kill all life stages of the beetle. Sieving can remove adult beetles and larvae from lightly infested grain. Store wheat in airtight containers or sealed metallic bins to prevent re-infestation. Cooling storage areas below 15°C significantly reduces beetle activity and reproduction.   Chemical Control - Fumigation: For heavily infested stored wheat, fumigation with Aluminum Phosphide 56% is highly effective. Use 03 tablets (03 gm) per ton or 150 gm per 100 m3 or 10 gm Pouch per ton of commodity or 150 gm per 100 m3 per tonne of stored grain. The tablets should be placed in the grain mass at different depths, and the storage structure must be sealed airtight with polythene sheets or tarpaulin for 5-7 days. Aluminum Phosphide releases phosphine gas, which is toxic to all life stages of storage pests.  Waiting period: One hour of partial aeration in case non-polyethylene packed commodities allowed by 6-8 hours of full aeration. For polyethylene packed commodities, minimum aeration period is 48 hrs. The waiting period for the release of stock is 48hrs in both the cases. Recommendation for bag stock 15 days  Deltamethrin 02.50 % WP @ 485 grams per acre (water required is 1 litre/30 square meters)   Critical Safety Precautions for Fumigation:  - Fumigation should ONLY be conducted by trained personnel or under expert supervision  - Ensure complete airtight sealing of the storage structure  - Display warning signs clearly indicating fumigation in progress  - Keep children, livestock, and unauthorized persons away from fumigation sites  - After fumigation period, open storage structure and aerate for at least 48-72 hours before handling grains  - Use protective equipment (gas masks, gloves) while handling fumigants  - Never consume or sell grain immediately after fumigation without proper aeration   Integrated Management Approach: For long-term storage (beyond 3-4 months), combine preventive measures with monitoring. Use pheromone traps to detect early infestation. Mix stored grain with food-grade diatomaceous earth (DE) at 1-2 kg per tonne, which damages the insect cuticle and causes dehydration. Mixing neem seed kernel powder (NSKP) at 2% (20 kg per tonne) provides botanical protection.   Important Note: Aluminum Phosphide is a highly toxic restricted-use pesticide. Its purchase and application are regulated under the Insecticides Act, 1968. Farmers must obtain proper authorization and training before use. For safe and effective storage pest management, please contact your nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) in Kushinagar district for expert guidance, training on fumigation techniques, and assistance in accessing certified pest control operators for large-scale storage facilities.

generated_question: My Berseem crop is getting damaged by some caterpillars, what can I do?
generated_answer: Some caterpillars like Bihar hairy caterpillar and gram caterpillar can harm your Berseem crop. These pests eat the plants and can destroy them completely. seed_question: What are the control measures for Bihar hairy caterpillar and gram caterpillar in Berseem crop?
seed_answer: Bihar hairy caterpillar and gram caterpillar are significant pests that can cause substantial damage to Berseem (Egyptian clover) crops, particularly during the vegetative growth stages. Understanding their biology and implementing integrated pest management strategies is crucial for effective control.   1. Bihar hairy caterpillar (Bhabu Kuta): It is polyphagous and sporadic pest. It attacks berseem crop twice in a season. - In September-October larvae devour the young crop to the ground level and the farmers have to resow it. Again, it feeds voraciously on plant foliage and causes severe losses in March-April.  Control: - Weeds like Gutpatna, Bathu, Jangli Palak and wild castor are the alternate host plants on which the pest feeds in gregarious phase and later on migrates to berseem. Destroy these weeds growing adjoining to berseem fields before its sowing.  2. Gram caterpillar (Helicoverpa armigera): The attacks of this pest is serious on seed crop. - Avoid raising of berseem seed crop adjoining to tomato, gram, late sown wheat, sathi moong, sathi mash and sunflower on which the pest multiplies and later shifts to berseem.  Control: - The pest should be controlled properly on the above mentioned crops grown in the vicinity of berseem in order to check its migration to berseem fields. - Spray Spinosad 45 % SC @60-62ml/acre at an economic threshold level.    For further guidance or clarification, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

These weeds are where the pests live and then move to your berseem. Also, keep your berseem away from crops like tomato, gram, and sunflower. seed_question: How can I control borer infestation in sugarcane?
seed_answer: "Borer infestation is one of the most economically damaging pest problems in sugarcane cultivation, significantly reducing both cane yield and sugar recovery. The primary borers affecting sugarcane include the early shoot borer (Chilo infuscatellus), top borer (Scirpophaga excerptalis), and internode borer (Chilo sacchariphagus indicus). These pests cause characteristic symptoms such as dead hearts in young plants, bunchy tops, and internal tunneling in mature canes, leading to substantial crop losses if left unmanaged.    **Pest Description and Damage Symptoms:**  Early shoot borer is particularly destructive during the initial growth stages, with larvae boring into the central shoot causing wilting and drying of the central leaf, known as 'dead heart.' The top borer attacks during the later growth stages, causing similar dead heart symptoms and bunchy top appearance. Internode borers create tunnels within the cane internodes, weakening the stem and reducing juice quality. Infested plants show stunted growth, reduced tillering, and in severe cases, complete crop failure in affected patches.    **Chemical Control Measures:**  For effective management of early shoot borer, soil application of **Chlorpyrifos 20% EC** is highly recommended as a primary control measure. Apply Chlorpyrifos 20% EC at the rate of 2.5 liters per hectare as a soil drench around the base of the plants during the early growth stage (30-45 days after planting). This systemic action provides protection during the vulnerable establishment phase. Alternatively, granular formulations like **Carbofuran 3% CG** at 33 kg per hectare or **Phorate 10% CG** at 10 kg per hectare can be applied in the soil at planting or as side dressing during earthing up operations.    **Safety Precautions and Protective Measures:**  When handling and applying organophosphate insecticides like Chlorpyrifos, farmers must wear complete protective equipment including long-sleeved shirts, long pants, chemical-resistant gloves, protective eyewear, and face masks or respirators. Avoid application during windy conditions and never eat, drink, or smoke during handling. Store chemicals in original containers away from food and animal feed. Observe a Pre-Harvest Interval (PHI) of at least 21-28 days before harvesting sugarcane for crushing to ensure chemical residues are within safe limits.    **Biological Control Measures:**  Integrated pest management should include biological control agents such as release of **Trichogramma chilonis** egg parasitoids at 50,000 per hectare during the early whorl stage, repeated at 10-day intervals for three releases. **Cotesia flavipes**, a larval parasitoid, is highly effective against internode borers and should be released at 2,500-5,000 parasitoids per hectare.    **Cultural Control Practices:**  Implement the following cultural practices: remove and destroy dead hearts and infested shoots regularly through detrashing; avoid ratooning heavily infested crops; maintain optimal spacing and avoid dense planting; practice crop rotation with non-host crops; use resistant or tolerant varieties when available; ensure proper irrigation and balanced fertilization to promote vigorous plant growth; and undertake timely earthing up operations which help expose and destroy borer larvae.    **Monitoring and Threshold Levels:**  Regular field monitoring is essential. Economic threshold for intervention is when 5-10% of tillers show dead heart symptoms during the vegetative stage. Early detection through weekly field scouting enables timely intervention and prevents severe infestations.    For further guidance or clarification, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer."

generated_question: Why are my sugarcane plants not producing enough shoots this season?
generated_answer: Your sugarcane might not be producing enough shoots because of short days, not enough water during the tillering stage, or a lack of nutrients like phosphorus, copper, or zinc. Poor irrigation, low phosphorus, or copper deficiency can also cause yellowing or discoloration of leaves and weak growth. Zinc deficiency can lead to yellow leaf blades and short stalks. To help, make sure you water enough during key growth stages, use 2% DAP foliar spray twice, and apply the right fertilizers. Test your soil before planting and use certified seeds to avoid diseases.
seed_question: What are the reasons for low sugarcane tillering or poor sprouting this time?
seed_answer: Poor tillering in sugarcane may occur due to several environmental, agronomic, deficiencies, diseases and management factors. Tillering is a critical stage in sugarcane growth, and any adverse condition during this stage reduces tiller production and ultimately affects yield.  Reasons for low tillering - Short winter days reduce tillering and produce thinner, elongated plants with lower sucrose content. - Inadequate irrigation during the tillering stage leads to reduced tiller formation. - Phosphorus deficiency reduces tillering and decreases the shoot-to-root ratio due to restricted root development. It also causes red or purple discoloration of leaf tips and margins, slender leaves, short and slender stalks, and poor or no tillering. - Short day length decreases the number of tillers. - Copper deficiency causes green splotches on leaves followed by bleaching. The stalk and meristems lose turgidity, and internodal length and tillering are reduced. - Zinc deficiency also leads to low tillering. Symptoms include yellowing of the leaf blade while the midrib and margins remain green, red lesions on leaves, shorter internodes, and reduced tillering. - Ratoon stunting disease also reduces tillering. Diseased clumps show stunted growth, reduced tillering, thin stalks with shortened internodes, and yellowish foliage. In infected canes, orange-red vascular bundles or yellowish discoloration at the nodes may be observed.  Management - Apply proper irrigation at critical stages of sugarcane growth. The total water requirement of sugarcane in Punjab is about 170–180 cm. If water is limited, irrigation should be prioritized at sprouting, tillering, and ripening stages. - Foliar spray of 2% DAP twice at a fortnight interval is helpful (20 g DAP in 1 litre of water). Also application of recommended dose of fertilizers at right time is suggested. - For zinc-deficient soils, apply 15 kg zinc sulphate per acre before the last ploughing. - To manage ratoon stunting disease, select setts from disease-free fields or certified nurseries and remove and burn infected clumps showing disease symptoms. - Farmer is advised to conduct a soil test before sowing and apply the essential nutrients in the form of fertilizers that are low in quantity in the soil to meet the demand of the crop For further guidance and clarification, the farmer is advised to visit the nearest KVK.

You can spray 2% DAP twice, a fortnight apart, and add zinc sulphate if needed. Also, use healthy setts and remove any infected plants.

Symptoms include yellowing of the leaf blade while the midrib and margins remain green, red lesions on leaves, shorter internodes, and reduced tillering. - Ratoon stunting disease also reduces tillering. Diseased clumps show stunted growth, reduced tillering, thin stalks with shortened internodes, and yellowish foliage. In infected canes, orange-red vascular bundles or yellowish discoloration at the nodes may be observed.  Management - Apply proper irrigation at critical stages of sugarcane growth. The total water requirement of sugarcane in Punjab is about 170–180 cm. generated_question: How often should I water my cotton plants and when is the best time to do it?
generated_answer: You should water your cotton plants 4 to 6 times depending on the rain. The first watering should be 4 to 6 weeks after planting, then every two or three weeks after that. If you have light soil or planted on ridges, you might need to water earlier. Make sure not to let the plants get too dry during flowering and fruiting, or they might drop flowers and bolls. Avoid watering in the middle of the day—early morning or evening is better. Drain any standing water if it happens. In the end, water by the end of September to help the bolls open. Don't overwater during boll opening, or it might hurt the quality of the cotton.
seed_question: What is the proper irrigation schedule and timing for cotton crop?
seed_answer: Irrigation management is critical for cotton cultivation as it directly impacts plant growth, yield, and fiber quality. Cotton requires adequate moisture at specific growth stages while avoiding waterlogging, which can severely damage the crop.   Cotton requires 4-6 irrigations depending upon the seasonal rainfall. The first irrigation should be given 4 to 6 weeks after sowing and the subsequent ones at interval of two or three weeks. However, on light soils or in crop sown on ridges, the first irrigation may be advanced, if necessary.   Sowing cotton on ridges and irrigation in furrows save considerable amount of water. Under poor quality irrigation water conditions, give pre sowing irrigation with canal water and subsequent irrigations can be applied with poor quality tube well water in alternate furrows.   In soils irrigated with saline water (EC upto 10 dS/m), application of 16 quintal per acre of rice-residue biochar reduces adverse effect of salinity and increases seed cotton yield. The crop must not be allowed to suffer from water stress during the flowering and fruiting stages, otherwise a lot of shedding of flowers and bolls will take place resulting in low yield. Cotton during its early growth is very sensitive to water stagnation. Therefore, drain out the stagnant water if such a situation arises. To hasten boll opening, give the last irrigation by the end of September.  American Bt cotton hybrids should be drip-irrigated at 7-day intervals using lateral pipes laid 67.5 cm apart, with drippers placed at 75 cm spacing and having a discharge rate of 2.2 litres per hour. Irrigation should be scheduled as follows: 50 minutes during May–June, 45 minutes in July, 40 minutes in August, and 35 minutes in September. Fertigation with 100 kg urea (equivalent to 45 kg nitrogen) per acre should be started at 35 days after sowing and completed within 110–120 days by applying it in 10 equal splits at 7-day intervals.  If the dripper discharge rate differs, the irrigation duration should be adjusted proportionally using the formula: Adjusted time (minutes) = (2.2 × prescribed irrigation time) ÷ dripper discharge (litres per hour).  Under conditions of limited availability of good-quality irrigation water, the alternate use of good-quality canal water and saline tubewell water through surface drip irrigation is recommended in light-textured soils to obtain sustainable seed-cotton yield with minimal adverse effects on soil quality.  Precautions:    - Avoid waterlogging as cotton roots are sensitive to excess moisture, leading to root rot and wilt diseases.  - Don't irrigate during peak afternoon heat; early morning or evening is ideal.  - Ensure proper drainage in fields to prevent water stagnation.  - Reduce irrigation during cloudy, humid weather to minimize disease pressure.  - In case of unexpected rains, skip the scheduled irrigation.   Proper irrigation at critical stages can increase cotton yield by 30-40% compared to rainfed cultivation. However, excess irrigation during boll opening stage can reduce fiber quality and increase pest problems like pink bollworm and whitefly.   For specific irrigation scheduling based on your soil type, weather conditions, and variety planted, contact your nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) in Bathinda for personalized guidance and soil moisture monitoring support.

generated_question: How do I make a waste decomposer for my wheat field?
generated_answer: To make a waste decomposer, mix 2 kg of jaggery in 200 liters of water in a plastic drum. Add one bottle of waste decomposer, stir with a wooden stick, and cover it with paper or cardboard. Stir once or twice a day. After five days, the solution will turn creamy and be ready to use. You can reuse the solution by adding 20 liters of it to a new drum with 2 kg of jaggery and 200 liters of water. This helps improve soil health and can be used on your wheat field.
seed_question: What is the method of making waste decomposer for wheat cultivation?
seed_answer: Waste decomposer is an effective bio-formulation that helps wheat farmers in Uttar Pradesh convert agricultural residues and organic waste into nutrient-rich compost quickly. This is particularly important for wheat cultivation, as it improves soil health, reduces stubble burning, and provides essential nutrients for the crop.  NCOF launched a waste decomposer culture in 2015, which is proving to be a miracle all over the country, used for quick composting from organic waste, soil health improvement, and as a plant protection agent. It is a consortium of microorganisms extracted from desi cow dung and is sold in 30-gram bottles for Rs. 20/- directly through NCOF and RCOF, a product now validated by ICAR. To prepare the solution, 2 kg of jaggery is mixed in a plastic drum with 200 liters of water, followed by the addition of one bottle of waste decomposer. After mixing properly with a wooden stick, the drum is covered with paper or cardboard and stirred once or twice daily; after five days, the solution turns creamy and is ready for use.  Farmers can sustain this culture for a lifetime by preparing it repeatedly; this is done by adding 20 liters of the existing solution to a drum containing 2 kg of jaggery and 200 liters of water. Regarding its uses, applying 1000 liters of waste decomposer per acre changes the biological and physical properties of all soil types, including acidic and alkaline soils, within 21 days. Furthermore, this application helps generate an earthworm population of up to 4 lakh in one acre of land within just six months.  For further guidance on waste decomposer preparation and application specific to your soil type and field conditions, please contact your nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) in Zamania block, Ghazipur district.

generated_question: What is Sagarika, and can it be used for my wheat crop?
generated_answer: Sagarika is a seaweed-based biostimulant made from red and brown algae. It helps improve wheat growth, root development, and stress resistance. It comes in liquid and granule forms and should be used along with regular fertilizers, not as a replacement. It helps with nutrient efficiency and makes plants stronger. You can use Sagarika Z++ granules at 10 kg per acre at sowing and again 15–20 days later. The liquid form is sprayed on leaves at 500 ml per acre. It’s good for soil health and plant vigor.
seed_question: What is the information regarding Quantis Biostimulant for wheat cultivation?

Sagarika is a seaweed-based biostimulant developed by IFFCO (Indian Farmers Fertilizer Cooperative) in collaboration with Aquagri Processing Pvt. Ltd. generated_question: My maize plants are looking sick, and the leaves are turning yellow. What's going on?
generated_answer: The yellowing leaves in your maize could be due to a lack of nitrogen or zinc. For nitrogen deficiency, older leaves turn yellow starting from the tip and spread in a V-shape. You can fix this by applying nitrogen fertilizer as recommended. For zinc deficiency, the leaves have a white stripe with red veins. You can add zinc to the soil at sowing or spray a zinc solution if the problem comes late.
seed_question: What causes yellowing of leaves in maize and how should it be managed?
seed_answer: Yellowing of leaves in maize is a common nutritional disorder that significantly affects crop health and yield potential. This condition, known as chlorosis, typically indicates nitrogen deficiency and zinc deficiency   Nitrogen Deficiency in Maize: Nitrogen deficiency in maize causes the older leaves to turn pale green to yellow, starting from the tip and spreading along the midrib in a V-shaped pattern. Plants become stunted, with poor growth and thin stems, reducing yield. To correct this deficiency, nitrogen fertiliser should be applied at the recommended rate. For varieties PMH-1, PMH-11, PMH-13, PMH-14, PMH-17, DKC 9144, Bioseed 9788, ADV 9293, JC-12 and Punjab Sweet Corn-1, apply 50 kg nitrogen per acre, which is equivalent to about 110 kg urea per acre. For PMH-2, JC-4 and Pearl Popcorn, apply 35 kg nitrogen per acre, which is equal to about 75 kg urea per acre. One-third of nitrogen, along with the entire quantity of phosphorus and potassium, should be drilled at the time of sowing. One-third nitrogen should be top-dressed at the knee-high stage and the remaining one-third at the pre-tasseling stage.   Zinc Deficiency in Maize: Zinc deficiency symptoms appear within 2 weeks of seedling emergence. A broad band of white or very light-yellow tissue, with reddish veins, appears on each side of the midrib, beginning at the base of the second or third leaf from the top of the plant. The white patch later extends in stripes towards the tip, parallel to the midrib. The midrib and the leaf margin remain green. The plants remain stunted and have short internodes. In the case of mild deficiency there is a white stripe in the upper leaves. The mild deficiency disappears by mid-season, but the silking and tasseling are delayed. Where zinc deficiency had been noticed in the preceding crop, broadcast 10 kg of zinc sulphate heptahydrate (21%) or 6.5 kg zinc sulphate monohydrate (33%) per acre at sowing mixed with an equal quantity of dry soil, along rows, hoe it into the soil and then irrigate the field. In a standing crop, apply 10 kg of zinc sulphate heptahydrate (21%) or 6.5 kg zinc sulphate monohydrate (33%) mixed with an equal quantity of dry soil along rows, hoe it into the soil and then irrigate the field. When the symptoms are observed late in the season, and interculture is not possible, spray a zinc sulphate-lime mixture prepared by mixing 1.2 kg of zinc sulphate heptahydrate (21%) and 0.6 kg of unslaked lime or 0.75 kg zinc sulphate monohydrate (33%) and 0.38 kg of unslaked lime with 200 litres of water to cover one acre.

generated_question: My maize plants are looking yellow, what could be the problem and how do I fix it?
generated_answer: Your maize plants turning yellow might be due to a lack of nitrogen or zinc. For nitrogen deficiency, older leaves turn yellow starting from the tip. Apply 50 kg nitrogen per acre for most varieties, which is about 110 kg urea. For some varieties, use 35 kg nitrogen per acre. Split the nitrogen into three parts—use one-third at sowing, one-third at the knee-high stage, and the rest before tasseling. For zinc deficiency, apply 10 kg zinc sulphate heptahydrate or 6.5 kg zinc sulphate monohydrate per acre at sowing. If the problem appears later, mix the zinc with soil and water it in. You can also spray a zinc and lime mix if needed.

generated_question: Why can't I use zinc and phosphorus in dry fields before planting paddy?
generated_answer: Zinc and phosphorus don't work well in dry soil before planting paddy. The plants can't take them in properly when the soil is dry. It's better to add them when the field is wet, like during the last land preparation. This helps the nutrients stay available for the plants. If you use them in dry soil, they might get stuck and not help the crop, which wastes money and nutrients.
seed_question: Why are zinc and phosphorous not recommended in dry fields before transplanting paddy?
seed_answer: Zinc and phosphorous fertilizers are not recommended for application in dry fields before transplanting paddy because their availability and uptake by plants are significantly reduced under dry soil conditions. These nutrients are best applied during the last puddling or land preparation, as waterlogged conditions enhance their availability and facilitate better absorption by rice plants. In dry conditions, these nutrients may get fixed in the soil and become unavailable to the crop, leading to wastage of fertilizers and poor plant nutrition. Therefore, for efficient use, P and Zn should be incorporated into the soil just before transplanting under suitable moisture conditions.

generated_question: My okra plants have white stuff on the leaves, what can I do?
generated_answer: The white stuff on your okra leaves is powdery mildew. It usually affects older leaves and stems. The spots grow and look like talcum powder. This happens more in humid weather. To control it, you can spray neem oil at 0.03% or use sulphur 80% WP or a mix of Tebuconazole and Trifloxystrobin. Make sure to keep the plants well fertilized and spaced properly to reduce humidity.
seed_question: What are the effective methods for controlling downy mildew in bhindi (okra)?
seed_answer: According to the Punjab Agricultural University Package of Practices (POP), NIPHM, PPQS, NHB, and other state advisories such as (HAU, TNAU), downy mildew is not considered a serious disease in okra crop, as its occurrence is generally limited and it does not cause significant economic loss under normal conditions.  However, okra crop is severely affected by mildew types i.e. powdery mildew.  Powdery Mildew Symptoms:  The disease is found mainly on the older leaves and stems of plants. Yields of many of the infected vegetables are reduced due to premature foliage loss. Increased humidity can increase the severity of the disease, and infection is enhanced during periods of heavy dew. The disease symptoms appear as subtle, small, round, whitish spots on leaves and sometimes stems. The spots enlarge and coalesce rapidly and a white mass resembling talcum powder becomes evident on the upper surface of older leaves or other plant parts. Young leaves are almost immune. A large part of the talc-like powder on the leaf surface is composed of spores. These spores are easily blown by winds to nearby susceptible plants. Heavily infected leaves become yellow, then become dry and brown. Extensive premature defoliation of the older leaves can ensue if the disease is not controlled.  Integrated Disease Management Approach:   Cultural control:  Monitor the adjacent field for infection. Maintain proper plant spacing for reducing the relative humidity. Healthy, vigorous leaves and stems are less prone to infection. Plants under nutritional stress in most cases will develop powdery mildew much sooner than plants the same age grown under good nutritional program. Hence the plant should be well manured, and application of fertilizers should be done on the basis of standard recommendations.  Biological control: Spray azadirachtin 0.03% (300 ppm) neem oil-based EC @ 800-1000 ml in 200 litres of water/acre.  Chemical control:  Spray sulphur 80% WP @ 1.2 kg in 300-400 litres of water per acre, or Tebuconazole 50% + Trifloxystrobin 25% WG @ 120-140 g per acre in 200 litres of water.      For further guidance or clarification, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

seed_question: What is the foliar spray treatment for zinc deficiency in paddy showing reddish leaves and drying plants?
seed_answer: For zinc deficiency in paddy showing reddish-brown leaves and drying symptoms, apply a foliar spray of 0.5% zinc sulphate heptahydrate solution by dissolving 500 g of zinc sulphate heptahydrate in 100 liters of water per acre. Alternatively, spray a 0.3% zinc sulphate monohydrate solution by dissolving 300 g of zinc sulphate monohydrate in 100 liters of water per acre. In highly deteriorated soils, the symptoms of zinc deficiency sometimes appear in patches even after the application of the recommended dose of zinc sulphate. In that event, broadcast 10 kg of zinc sulphate heptahydrate or 6.5 kg of zinc sulphate monohydrate per acre mixed with an equal quantity of dry soil on the affected patches.

generated_question: My paddy seedlings got hit by hail, and now they look all torn up. What can I do?
generated_answer: After a hailstorm, check your paddy seedlings for torn leaves, broken stems, or bruised areas. These injuries can let in fungus or bacteria. Drain any water from the nursery beds right away and remove the worst damaged plants. Gently straighten the ones that are bent. You can also spray a fungicide like Carbendazim or Mancozeb to protect the seedlings from infection. If the problem is bad, you might need to start over with new seedlings.
seed_question: How to manage hailstorm damage in paddy nursery?
seed_answer: Hailstorm damage in paddy nurseries is a critical concern that requires immediate attention and proper management to prevent secondary infections and ensure healthy seedling recovery. While hailstorms cause physical injury rather than disease, the wounds created make seedlings highly susceptible to fungal and bacterial infections, particularly in the humid conditions of nursery beds.  Understanding Hailstorm Impact: Hailstorm damage in paddy nurseries manifests as torn leaves, broken stems, bruised seedlings, and physical punctures in plant tissue. These injuries create entry points for pathogens, especially fungal diseases like damping-off, seedling blight, and bacterial leaf streak. The severity depends on hailstone size, storm duration, and seedling age. Young seedlings (7-15 days old) are more vulnerable than older, established ones.  Immediate Post-Hailstorm Management: Within 24-48 hours after a hailstorm, farmers should drain excess water from nursery beds to prevent waterlogging, which promotes fungal growth. Remove severely damaged, broken, or completely destroyed seedlings to reduce disease spread. Gently straighten bent seedlings and support them with soil. Apply a light irrigation to wash off debris and mud splashes from leaf surfaces.  Preventive Fungicide Application: To prevent fungal infections through hailstorm wounds, apply Carbendazim 50% WP at 100-150 grams per acre or Mancozeb 75% WP at 400 grams per acre as a protective spray within 48 hours of the hailstorm. These broad-spectrum fungicides create a protective barrier against multiple fungal pathogens. Alternatively, apply Copper Oxychloride 50% WP at 500 grams per acre mixed in 200 liters of water for bacterial disease prevention.  Long-term Prevention: For future seasons, consider establishing nurseries in protected locations with natural windbreaks or tree lines. Use protective netting or shade nets during vulnerable periods if hailstorms are common in the region during nursery season.  For further guidance or clarification on hailstorm damage management, pest identification, or appropriate chemical recommendations specific to Haryana conditions, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer in Panchkula district.

seed_question: What is the recommended fertilizer schedule for irrigated wheat cultivation in Uttar Pradesh?
seed_answer: Proper fertilizer management is crucial for achieving optimal wheat yields in the irrigated areas of Uttar Pradesh. A balanced nutrient approach ensures healthy crop growth, better tillering, and improved grain quality.    Recommended Fertilizer Doses for Irrigated Wheat (Per Acre):    For irrigated wheat cultivation in Uttar Pradesh, the standard fertilizer recommendation is:    - Nitrogen (N): 60 kg per acre, supplied through approximately 130 kg of Urea  - Phosphorus (P₂O₅): 24 kg per acre, supplied through approximately 150 kg of Single Super Phosphate (SSP)  - Potassium ((K₂O): 16 kg per acre, supplied through approximately 27 kg of Muriate of Potash (MOP)    Application Method and Timing:    The split application of fertilizers is essential for maximizing nutrient use efficiency:    At Sowing Time (Basal Application):  - Apply 50% of the nitrogen dose (approximately 65 kg Urea)  - Apply 100% of the phosphorus dose (150 kg SSP)  - Apply 100% of the potassium dose (27 kg MOP)  - Mix these fertilizers thoroughly and apply uniformly in the soil during final land preparation or place them in furrows at the time of sowing  - Ensure proper seed-fertilizer placement to avoid seed damage    At First Irrigation (Crown Root Initiation Stage - 21-25 days after sowing):  - Apply the remaining 50% nitrogen (approximately 65 kg Urea)  - This application coincides with the critical Crown Root Initiation (CRI) stage when wheat plants develop their permanent root system  - Apply the fertilizer uniformly and irrigate immediately for better nutrient absorption    Additional Nutrient Considerations:   Zinc Deficiency Management: If your field shows zinc deficiency symptoms (yellowing between leaf veins, stunted growth), apply 10 kg Zinc Sulphate per acre mixed with the basal dose. For severe deficiency, spray 0.5% Zinc Sulphate solution (500 grams in 100 liters of water) at 25-30 days after sowing.   Sulphur Management: Using SSP provides both phosphorus and sulphur (12% S content). However, if using DAP instead of SSP, consider adding 8-10 kg elemental sulphur per acre or use gypsum (50 kg per acre) to meet sulphur requirements.   For High-Yielding Varieties: If you're growing high-yielding varieties like HD-2967, HD-3086, or DBW-187, consider increasing nitrogen to 80 kg per acre (approximately 175 kg Urea), especially in highly fertile soils with good irrigation facilities.   Soil Testing Recommendation: These are general recommendations. For precise fertilizer management, conduct soil testing every 2-3 years through your nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or soil testing laboratory. Soil test-based fertilizer application can save costs while improving yields by 15-20%.   Important Precautions:  - Avoid fertilizer application during waterlogged conditions  - Never apply fertilizers on dry soil without irrigation  - Store fertilizers in cool, dry places away from direct sunlight  - Use protective equipment while handling fertilizers   For further guidance on soil testing, fertilizer subsidies, or customized nutrient management for your specific soil type and wheat variety, please contact your nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) in Badaun district or consult the district agriculture office.

generated_question: How far apart should I plant the maize rows to get the most fodder?
generated_answer: You should plant the maize rows about 30 cm apart. This helps the plants grow better and gives more green fodder. Use around 30 kg of seed per acre. You can plant from early March to mid-September, but it's better to plant between mid-April and mid-August to avoid pests. You can use a drill or the kera method. You can also grow it with cowpea by mixing 15 kg of maize seed with 6 kg of cowpea seed per acre. It works well even without tilling the soil.
seed_question: What is the recommended row-to-row spacing for planting fodder maize to maximize forage yield?
seed_answer: The recommended row-to-row spacing for planting fodder maize to maximize forage yield is about 30 cm between rows. This spacing helps maintain optimum plant population, better sunlight use, and higher green fodder yield.  For best results, use about 30 kg of seed per acre. Maize can be sown from the first week of March to mid-September, but to reduce the risk of fall armyworm, it is better to sow between mid-April and mid-August. The crop can be sown by the kera or pora method, or with a seed-cum-fertilizer drill for proper placement of seed and fertilizer.  Fodder maize can also be grown as a mixed crop with cowpea by using 15 kg of maize seed along with 6 kg of cowpea (CL 367) seed per acre. It can be successfully grown under no-tillage conditions, also giving similar green fodder yield as conventional tillage. With proper management, you can take up to three fodder crops from the same field in a year.

generated_question: My barseem crop is getting choked with weeds, what can I do?
generated_answer: If weeds are a problem, you can mix barseem with raya when you sow. Raya grows fast and covers the ground, which helps stop weeds from growing. If the weeds are really bad, wait until the second week of October to sow. The weeds will be less then because it gets cooler. There's no specific herbicide recommended for barseem, so focus on planting methods to keep weeds under control.
seed_question: What are the safety precautions and best practices for spraying herbicides in barseem (Egyptian clover) cultivation?
seed_answer: Herbicide application in crops requires careful attention to safety protocols, timing and application techniques to ensure effective weed control while minimizing crop damage and environmental risks. Although, according to the PAU POP of barseem their is no recommendation for the herbicide application to control weeds.  Weed management : When itsit (Trianthema portulacastrum) becomes a problem, berseem should be sown in mixture with raya, a fast-growing crop that suppresses this weed effectively through its strong smothering effect. In fields where the infestation is severe, sowing should be postponed until the second week of October, because the occurrence of this weed declines significantly during this period due to the drop in temperature.   For further guidance or clarification, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

generated_question: My pea plants are getting yellow leaves at the bottom, what's going on?
generated_answer: The yellowing of lower leaves in your pea plants is a sign of wilt disease. This happens because of root rot caused by fungi like Fusariumoxysporum and Rhizoctonia solani. The leaves turn yellow, and the plants may get stunted. The stems might look swollen and brittle near the soil. To manage this, treat your seeds with Carbendazim 50% WP or Captan before sowing. Also, use the right amount of fertilizer based on soil testing. You can add 8 tonnes of farmyard manure and 20 kg of nitrogen and 25 kg of phosphorus per acre. If you're planting peas for the first time in an area, treat the seeds with Rhizobiumleguminosarum to help them grow better.
seed_question: How can I control the yellowing of lower leaves in pea plants?
seed_answer: Yellowing of lower leaves in pea plants is a symptom observed in wilt disease.   Causes and Symptoms: Wilt, root rot (Fusariumoxysporum andRhizoctonia solani) Root rotting and yellowing of lower leaves, followed by wilting.  of lower leaves and stunting of plants. Wilt: Yellowing of lower leaves and stunting of plants. The xylem vessels develop brown discoloration and get distorted. Leaflet margins curl downward and inward The stem may be slightly swollen and brittle near the soil. Internal woody stem tissue often is discolored, turning lemon brown to orange brown.Externally, the root system appears healthy; however, secondary root rots are likely to occur on plants wilted for long periods. Eventually, wilted plants may die.  Management: - Avoid early sowing in badly infested areas. -  Treat the seed with 15 g Talc based formulation of Pseudomonas fluorescens per kg seed before sowing -  For wilt control, treat the seed with Carbendazim 50% WP 1 g or Captan 2 g per kg of seed.  Nutrient Management: Maintain balanced fertilization with recommended doses of NPK based on soil testing. For peas,  Apply 8 tonnes of farmyard manure,20 kg of N (45 kg Urea) and 25 kg P2O5 (155 kg Superphosphate)per acre before sowing.  In areas where pea crop has not been sown earlier,it is advisable to treat the seed with bacterial culture (Rhizobiumleguminosarum) to ensure nodule formation and quick growth. It increases the yield and quality of pods. The culture is available in the Department of Microbiology, Punjab Agricultural University, Ludhiana. One acre culture packet should be mixed with half litre of water. Rub the mixture thoroughly on seed to give a fine covering of the culture to every seed. Thereafter, spread the seed in shade for drying and sow it immediately afterwards  For further guidance or clarification, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

generated_answer: The yellowing of lower leaves in pea plants is a sign of wilt disease. This happens due to root rot caused by fungi like Fusarium oxysporum and Rhizoctonia solani. The xylem vessels in the plant turn brown and get distorted. To manage this, treat the seeds with Carbendazim 50% WP or Captan before sowing. Also, use 8 tonnes of farmyard manure and the right amounts of nitrogen and phosphorus. You can treat the seeds with Rhizobium leguminosarum to help the plants grow better. Make sure to mix the culture properly with water and dry the seeds before sowing.

generated_question: I heard using too much DAP might hurt my maize. Is that true?
generated_answer: Yes, using too much DAP can be bad for your maize. It can mess up the balance of nutrients in the soil and make the plants sick. Too much phosphorus can stop the plants from getting other important nutrients like zinc and iron. This can cause yellowing leaves and slow growth. It's also bad for the soil in the long run and can pollute water. Always follow the recommended amounts. If you're not sure, get your soil tested first.
seed_question: Can we use an overdose of DAP fertilizer higher than the recommended rate in maize crop?
seed_answer: Using an overdose of DAP (Di-Ammonium Phosphate) fertilizer beyond the recommended rate in maize crop is not advisable and can lead to several adverse effects on crop health, soil fertility, and environmental sustainability. Understanding the proper fertilizer management practices is essential for achieving optimal maize yield while maintaining soil health.   Nutrient Requirement of Maize: Maize is a heavy feeder crop that requires balanced nutrition throughout its growth cycle. The recommended dose of fertilizers for maize under irrigated condition according to varieties : - PMH 1, 11, 13, 14 & 17, DKC 9144, Bioseed 9788, ADV 9293, JC 12 and  Punjab Sweet Corn 1 : N - 50 kg/acre  P- 24 kg/acres and K -12 kg/acres. DAP- 55 kg/acres. - PMH 2, JC 4 and  Pearl Popcorn: N - 35 kg/acres , P - 12kg/acres and  K-8 kg/acres and DAP is 27 kg/acres. - To all recommended varieties, drill  entire quantity of phosphorus at the time of sowing.  - When 27 kg of DAP is used, reduce the urea dose by 10 kg and when 55 kg of DAP is used, reduce the dose of urea by 20 kg.   Consequences of DAP Overdose:    1. Nutrient Imbalance: Excessive DAP application creates an imbalance in the NPK ratio. High phosphorus levels can interfere with the uptake of micronutrients, particularly zinc and iron, leading to induced deficiencies. Maize is particularly sensitive to zinc deficiency, which manifests as interveinal chlorosis on younger leaves and stunted growth.    2. Soil Health Deterioration: Continuous overdose of phosphatic fertilizers leads to phosphorus build-up in soil, which can alter soil pH and reduce the availability of other essential nutrients. This creates long-term soil fertility issues.    3. Environmental Pollution: Excess phosphorus not utilized by plants can leach into groundwater or run off into surface water bodies, contributing to eutrophication and water quality degradation.    4. Economic Loss: Applying fertilizer beyond crop requirement represents wasteful expenditure without proportionate yield increase, reducing farm profitability.   Recommended Fertilizer Management Practices:   Soil Testing: Before any fertilizer application, farmers should conduct soil testing to determine existing nutrient status. This helps in making informed decisions about fertilizer doses tailored to specific field conditions.   Micronutrient Management: In zinc-deficient soils (common in Punjab), Where zinc deficiency had been noticed in the preceding crop, broadcast 10 kg of zinc sulphate heptahydrate (21%) or 6.5 kg zinc sulphate monohydrate (33%) per acre at sowing mixed with an equal quantity of dry soil, along rows, hoe it into the soil and then irrigate the field. In standing crop, apply 10 kg of zinc sulphate heptahydrate (21%) or 6.5 kg zinc sulphate monohydrate (33%) mixed with an equal quantity of dry soil along rows, hoe it into the soil and then irrigate the field.    When the symptoms are observed late in the season and interculture is not possible, spray zinc sulphate-lime mixture prepared by mixing 1.2 kg of zinc sulphate heptahydrate (21%) and 0.6 kg of unslaked lime or 0.75 kg zinc sulphate monohydrate (33%) and 0.38 kg of unslaked lime with 200 litres of water to cover one acre zinc sulphate @ 10 kg/acre as soil application or spray 0.5% zinc sulphate solution at 30 and 45 days after sowing.   Safety Precautions: While handling chemical fertilizers, farmers should wear protective gloves and avoid direct skin contact. Store fertilizers in cool, dry places away from food items and keep out of reach of children. Wash hands thoroughly after handling.   Conclusion: Adhering to recommended fertilizer doses based on soil testing ensures optimal maize productivity, maintains soil health, and prevents environmental contamination. Over-application of DAP or any fertilizer does not guarantee higher yields and often results in diminishing returns.   For further guidance or clarification on fertilizer management practices specific to your field conditions, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

generated_question: My wheat plants are looking sick, and the leaves are yellowing. What's going on?
generated_answer: It sounds like you might have aphids, also called 'chepa.' These tiny insects suck the sap from your wheat plants, causing yellowing and curling of leaves. They also leave a sticky coating that can lead to mold. To control them, you can use insecticides like Malathion or Thiamethoxam. Make sure to spray when the aphid count gets high, and avoid using too much nitrogen in your fertilizer. Also, consider planting resistant wheat varieties.
seed_question: How to control aphids (chepa) in wheat crop?
seed_answer: Aphids, commonly known as 'chepa' in Hindi, are one of the most damaging sucking pests affecting wheat crops in Haryana and other wheat-growing regions of India. These small, soft-bodied insects can cause significant yield losses if not managed properly during the critical growth stages of wheat.   Visible Symptoms and Damage: Aphid infestation causes multiple problems in wheat crops. Infested plants show yellowing and curling of leaves, particularly on younger foliage. The pests suck plant sap, causing stunted growth and reduced tillering. Heavily infested plants develop a sticky honeydew coating on leaves, which promotes the growth of black sooty mold fungus, further reducing photosynthetic efficiency. During the grain-filling stage, aphid feeding leads to shriveled, lightweight grains with reduced quality and yield losses of 20-40% in severe cases. The pest also acts as a vector for viral diseases like Barley Yellow Dwarf Virus (BYDV).  These pests multiply rapidly under favorable conditions, with peak infestation occurring during February-March when temperatures range between 20-25°C.   Control Measures: Cultural Control: Implement several preventive measures to reduce aphid incidence. Avoid early sowing of wheat, as early-sown crops are more susceptible to aphid buildup. Remove alternate host plants and weeds from field borders. Maintain balanced fertilization, avoiding excessive nitrogen, which promotes succulent growth attractive to aphids. Ensure proper irrigation management, as water-stressed plants are more vulnerable. Grow resistant or tolerant wheat varieties like HD-2967, WH-1105, or DBW-88 that show reduced susceptibility to aphids.  Biological Control: Encourage natural enemies of aphids, including ladybird beetles (Coccinella septempunctata), lacewings (Chrysoperla carnea), and parasitic wasps (Aphidius species). These beneficial insects can provide long-term suppression of aphid populations. Avoid indiscriminate use of broad-spectrum insecticides that harm natural enemies.  Chemical Control: Give a seed treatment of Imidacloprid 48 % FS @ 0.14 kg per 100 kg of seeds. For immediate and effective control of heavy aphid populations, apply Malathion 50% EC @ 400 ml in 250 lit. of water or Thiamethoxam 25% WG @ 20 grams per acre (50 g/hectare), PHI-21 Days in 200 L of water, providing excellent systemic action. Or Quinalphos 25 % EC @ 400 ml per acre in 200-400 litres of water. Apply insecticides when aphid population reaches economic threshold level (10-15 aphids per earhead). For best results, spray during early morning or late evening hours when temperatures are cooler. Ensure thorough coverage of both upper and lower leaf surfaces. Repeat application after 10-15 days if infestation persists. Malathian 50 mg mix it in 250 liters of water and sprinkle it on the crop per acre .  For further guidance or clarification, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

seed_question: How to control aphids (chepa) in wheat crop?
seed_answer: Aphids, commonly known as 'chepa' in Hindi, are one of the most damaging sucking pests affecting wheat crops in Haryana and other wheat-growing regions of India. These small, soft-bodied insects can cause significant yield losses if not managed properly during the critical growth stages of wheat.   Visible Symptoms and Damage: Aphid infestation causes multiple problems in wheat crops. Infested plants show yellowing and curling of leaves, particularly on younger foliage. The pests suck plant sap, causing stunted growth and reduced tillering. Heavily infested plants develop a sticky honeydew coating on leaves, which promotes the growth of black sooty mold fungus, further reducing photosynthetic efficiency. During the grain-filling stage, aphid feeding leads to shriveled, lightweight grains with reduced quality and yield losses of 20-40% in severe cases. The pest also acts as a vector for viral diseases like Barley Yellow Dwarf Virus (BYDV).  These pests multiply rapidly under favorable conditions, with peak infestation occurring during February-March when temperatures range between 20-25°C.   Control Measures: Cultural Control: Implement several preventive measures to reduce aphid incidence. Avoid early sowing of wheat, as early-sown crops are more susceptible to aphid buildup. Remove alternate host plants and weeds from field borders. Maintain balanced fertilization, avoiding excessive nitrogen, which promotes succulent growth attractive to aphids. Ensure proper irrigation management, as water-stressed plants are more vulnerable. Grow resistant or tolerant wheat varieties like HD-2967, WH-1105, or DBW-88 that show reduced susceptibility to aphids.  Biological Control: Encourage natural enemies of aphids, including ladybird beetles (Coccinella septempunctata), lacewings (Chrysoperla carnea), and parasitic wasps (Aphidius species). These beneficial insects can provide long-term suppression of aphid populations. Avoid indiscriminate use of broad-spectrum insecticides that harm natural enemies.  Chemical Control: Give a seed treatment of Imidacloprid 48 % FS @ 0.14 kg per 100 kg of seeds. For immediate and effective control of heavy aphid populations, apply Malathion 50% EC @ 400 ml in 250 lit. of water or Thiamethoxam 25% WG @ 20 grams per acre (50 g/hectare), PHI-21 Days in 200 L of water, providing excellent systemic action. Or Quinalphos 25 % EC @ 400 ml per acre in 200-400 litres of water. Apply insecticides when aphid population reaches economic threshold level (10-15 aphids per earhead). For best results, spray during early morning or late evening hours when temperatures are cooler. Ensure thorough coverage of both upper and lower leaf surfaces. Repeat application after 10-15 days if infestation persists. Malathian 50 mg mix it in 250 liters of water and sprinkle it on the crop per acre . generated_question: What is the Fasal Bima Yojana all about?
generated_answer: Fasal Bima Yojana is a government crop insurance scheme that helps farmers get covered against losses from bad weather, pests, and diseases. It’s a low-cost insurance that covers crops from sowing to harvesting. Rabi crops like wheat have a premium of 1.5%. Farmers who take loans through Kisan Credit Cards can join, but now it’s optional. Even those without loans can apply. You can sign up online at pmfby.gov.in or at local offices like CSCs, KVK, or banks.
seed_question: Information regarding Fasal Bima Yojana
seed_answer: Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY) is the Government of India’s flagship crop insurance scheme, started in Kharif 2016 to give farmers affordable insurance cover against yield loss from sowing to post‑harvest. It provides compensation to farmers for crop losses due to drought, flood, hailstorm, pests, and diseases. The scheme offers crop insurance at a very low premium and covers the crop from sowing to harvesting stages. Under PMFBY, the premium rate for Rabi crops such as wheat, mustard, and barley is 1.5%. All farmers who take crop loans through the Kisan Credit Card (KCC) scheme are eligible; earlier enrolment was compulsory for loanee farmers, but it is now voluntary, and non-loanee farmers can also apply. Farmers can apply online through the official portal pmfby.gov.in or at Common Service Centres (CSCs). Offline applications can be submitted at the Agriculture Department office, KVK or ATMA offices, and banks in the case of loanee farmers.

generated_question: How should I treat my moong seeds before planting in the summer?
generated_answer: You should soak your moong seeds and treat them with a biofertilizer called Rhizobium. Mix one packet of the culture with the seeds after moistening them with about 300 mL of water. Let the seeds dry in the shade and sow them within an hour. This helps the plants fix nitrogen and grow better. You can get the Rhizobium culture from Punjab Agricultural University or your local KVK.
seed_question: What is the recommended seed soaking treatment for summer moong cultivation?
seed_answer: Seed soaking and treatment before sowing is a critical practice for successful summer moong (green gram) cultivation, particularly in Punjab's Fazilka region. This pre-sowing practice combines disease protection, biological nitrogen fixation enhancement, and improved germination rates.   Importance of Seed Treatment in Summer Moong: Summer moong, sown during March-April in Punjab, faces unique challenges including soil-borne pathogens, seed-borne diseases, and the need for efficient nitrogen fixation. Proper seed treatment addresses these challenges and establishes a strong foundation for the crop.   Recommended Seed Soaking and Treatment Protocol: - The Punjab Package of Practices (POP) recommends seed treatment with Rhizobium. Inoculate the seed with one packet of consortium biofertilizer (Rhizobium sp. LSMR-1 and Rhizobacterium RB-3) per acre at the time of sowing. Moisten the seed with about 300 mL of water, mix the seed thoroughly with the culture, and allow it to dry in the shade. Sow the seed within one hour of application of the biofertilizer. Inoculation of seed with consortium biofertilizer enhances grain yield. This biofertilizer is available at the Punjab Agricultural University Seed Shop, Gate No. 1, Ludhiana, and at Krishi Vigyan Kendras (KVKs)/Farm Advisory Service Centres in different districts.   For region-specific guidance on seed treatment protocols, Rhizobium culture procurement, recommended moong varieties for summer season, and soil testing facilities, please contact your nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK). KVK experts can provide location-specific recommendations based on your soil type and local conditions, ensuring optimal crop establishment and yield in summer moong cultivation.

Yeh karyavadan seed ki germination aur nitrogen fixation me madad karega.

generated_question: My paddy field has salty soil, what can I do to fix it?
generated_answer: To fix salty soil in your paddy field, add organic matter like paddy or wheat straw. Mix it into the soil up to 15–20 cm deep in April or May. Water it 2–3 times to help break down the residues and wash away salts. Also, plow deep to improve drainage and use green manure like dhaincha before planting. If the soil is very salty, use gypsum as recommended by a soil test. Keep adding organic matter over the years and choose salt-tolerant paddy varieties.
seed_question: What are the effective management practices for saline soil in paddy cultivation?
seed_answer: Effective management of saline (kallar) soils in paddy cultivation focuses on improving soil structure, leaching excess salts, and restoring productivity. The most economical and effective practice is the regular addition of organic matter, especially crop residues like paddy straw, wheat straw, and other plant materials. Incorporating these residues into the soil improves aggregation, increases water infiltration, and helps wash salts below the root zone. During decomposition, organic matter releases organic acids that reduce the harmful effects of salts. In Punjab, including areas like Bathinda, the best time to incorporate residues is April–May after wheat harvest and before paddy transplanting. Spread residues uniformly, mix them into the soil up to 15–20 cm using a disc harrow or rotavator, and apply 2–3 irrigations to aid decomposition and salt leaching. Additional important practices include deep ploughing (30–40 cm) to break hard pans and improve drainage, green manuring with dhaincha (Sesbania) before transplanting to add organic matter and nitrogen, and proper water management by maintaining standing water in the field so salts remain dissolved and do not accumulate near the roots. Gypsum application can be used where sodium salinity is high; apply based on soil test results, preferably along with organic matter, as gypsum supplies calcium that replaces sodium and improves soil structure. Farmers should also prefer salt-tolerant paddy varieties and continue organic matter addition over multiple seasons for lasting improvement. For kallar soil reclamation and testing, dig a 3-foot-deep pit with one side vertical and the other slanting. From the vertical side, collect about ½ kg of soil by removing a 1-inch layer from depths of 0–6, 6–12, 12–24, and 24–36 inches. Keep each depth sample in a separate clean cloth bag, properly labelled with field number, depth, farmer’s name, address, and sampling date, and send for soil analysis to guide precise reclamation measures.
