generated_question: I think I heard about a fertilizer called 20:20:13 for my maize. Is that right, and can I spray it on the leaves?
generated_answer: The fertilizer 20:20:13 doesn't exist. You might be thinking of 20:20:0:13, which gives nitrogen, phosphorus, and sulfur but no potassium. This fertilizer is meant for soil application, not spraying on leaves. You should apply it during sowing by broadcasting. Don't mix it with standing crops. If your crop shows sulfur deficiency, you can use magnesium sulfate at 1% (10 grams in 1 liter water). Always do a soil test before applying any fertilizer.
seed_question: What is the recommended foliar spray composition of NPK 20:20:13 and magnesium Sulphate per acre for maize crop?
seed_answer: There is no fertilizer named 20:20:13. However, farmer must have mistaken it with 20:20:0:13 fertilizer. 20:20:0:13 supplied nitrogen, phosphorus and sulphur. 100kg of this fertilizer is said to supply 20kg Nitrogen, 20kg phosphorus and 13kg sulphur to the field. This fertilizer is not said to supply potassium fertilizer.  NP(S) 20-20-13 should be applied to the soil considering important factors such as placement, proportion and time of crop cycle. It should be applied during sowing and through broadcasting. The dosage should be as per the crop and the soil (As per general recommendation for the State). It is advised not to use the NP(S) 20-20-0-13 with standing crops, application of NP(S) 20-20-0-13 through seed- cum fertilizer gives better outputs. There is no mention of its usage as foliar spray. However, since it is strictly advised to be applied as soil application, farmers are recommended not to use this fertilizer for foliar spray.  Magnesium sulphate fertilizer is suggested @ 1% (10 gm in 1 liter water) when sulphur deficiency symptoms are observed on the crop.  The farmer is advised to apply fertilizers after conducting a soil test. Based on the results of the soil test, the farmer should determine the quantity of fertilizers to be applied. The farmer is also strictly advised to apply fertilizers only in the prescribed method and in the recommended quantity.  Applying soil-applied fertilizers through foliar spray, or the indiscriminate use of fertilizers as foliar sprays without the occurrence of any deficiency symptoms, may cause phytotoxic effects on the crop or reduce fertilizer efficiency, thereby increasing the cost of cultivation.  For further guidance on foliar nutrition, soil testing, deficiency symptom diagnosis, or clarification on fertilizer recommendations specific to your field conditions, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer. They can provide customized nutrient management plans based on soil analysis and crop requirements.

generated_question: What's the right amount of fertilizer to use for my mustard crop?
generated_answer: You should use about 1.2 tonnes of well-decomposed FYM per acre before sowing. At sowing time, apply 18 kg urea, 101 kg SSP, and 27 kg MOP per acre. SSP helps with root growth, while urea and MOP give nitrogen and potash. For top dressing, use 35 kg urea at the branching stage and 18 kg urea at the flowering stage. seed_question: Asking about the basal fertilizer dose and top-dressing fertilizer dose in mustard cultivation
seed_answer: In mustard cultivation, basal and top-dressing fertilizer application should be done preferably on a soil test basis. For obtaining good growth and a higher yield in mustard, the proper use of basal and top-dressing fertilizers is very important. Apply about 1.2 tonnes of well-decomposed FYM per acre around 10 days before sowing. At the time of sowing, apply 18 kg urea, 101 kg SSP and 27 kg MOP per acre as a basal dose. SSP supplies phosphorus only, which helps in strong root development and early crop establishment, while urea and MOP meet the nitrogen and potash requirements. For top dressing, apply 35 kg urea per acre at the branching stage, followed by 18 kg urea per acre at the flowering stage, which supports better branching, flowering and higher yield in mustard. Apply at least two irrigations at the branching and flowering stages. In sulfur-deficient soil, apply 18 kg per acre.

Apply about 1.2 tonnes of well-decomposed FYM per acre around 10 days before sowing. At the time of sowing, apply 18 kg urea, 101 kg SSP and 27 kg MOP per acre as a basal dose. SSP supplies phosphorus only, which helps in strong root development and early crop establishment, while urea and MOP meet the nitrogen and potash requirements. For top dressing, apply 35 kg urea per acre at the branching stage, followed by 18 kg urea per acre at the flowering stage, which supports better branching, flowering and higher yield in mustard. Apply at least two irrigations at the branching and flowering stages. In sulfur-deficient soil, apply 18 kg per acre.

generated_question: What's the right amount of fertilizer to use when planting sugarcane?
generated_answer: At planting time, sugarcane needs a good mix of nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium. A general recommendation is 60 kg of nitrogen, which is about 130 kg of urea, for planting. If the soil is low in phosphorus and potassium, add 12 kg of phosphorus and 30 kg of potassium per acre. Apply all the phosphorus and potassium right at planting. For ratoon crops, give one-third of the nitrogen in February. If the soil is dry, apply half the nitrogen at planting and the rest when it rains. Fertilizers should go in the furrow, mixed with the soil, and kept 5-7 cm away from the sugarcane sets to avoid burning. You can also add 8 tonnes of farmyard manure per acre before planting.
seed_question: What is the recommended fertilizer dose for sugarcane at the time of planting?
seed_answer: Fertiliser application at the time of sugarcane planting is crucial for establishing a healthy crop stand and ensuring optimal cane yield and quality. The fertiliser dose varies based on soil type, nutrient status, variety, and regional agro-climatic conditions, but general recommendations provide a solid foundation for most growing areas.   General Fertiliser Dose Recommendations: At the time of planting, sugarcane requires a balanced application of macronutrients Nitrogen (N), Phosphorus (P), and Potassium (K). The standard recommendation for sugarcane is approximately 60 kg of nitrogen (130 kg of Urea) for Planting and 90 kg of nitrogen (195 kg of Urea) for ratoon. If the soil shows deficiencies of Phosphorus and Potassium use 12 kg of phosphorus (75 kg of SSP) and 30 kg of Potassium (50 kg of Muriate of Potash) per acre for the entire crop cycle. However, the application is split across different growth stages for optimal nutrient use efficiency.   Basal Application (At Planting Time): Apply all the Phosphorus and Potash at the time of Planting. For Ratoon Crop: Top-dress one-third of the nitrogen in February with the first hoeing. Rainfed Crop: If the moisture in the soil is optimum, apply one-half of the dose of nitrogen at planting. In case the moisture is deficient, the whole dose should be applied with the onset of rains   Application Method: Fertilisers should be applied in the furrow at the time of planting, mixed thoroughly with the soil, and covered before placing the seed setts. This ensures that developing roots have immediate access to essential nutrients, particularly phosphorus, which has limited mobility in soil. Placement 5-7 cm away from the setts prevents fertiliser burn while maintaining nutrient availability.   Organic Amendments:  Apply 8 tonnes of FYM/press-mud per acre 15 days before planting and mix into the soil with a plough or tiller. In case of trench planting, apply press-mud at the base of the trench and mix it into the soil with a kasola. In case FYM/press-mud is applied, use 40 (90 kg urea) kg N per acre. However, on coarse textured soilsif FYM is applied along with the recommended dose of nitrogen, approximately 10% higher yields can be obtained. On sandy soils, nitrogenous fertiliser may be applied after irrigation in moist soil, and the number of splits may be increased. Application of Azotobacter/Consortium bio-fertiliser @ 4 kg per acre in the furrows at the time of planting would help increase the cane yield. If FYM is in limited quantity, apply 4 kg consortiumbio-fertiliser with 4 tonnes of FYM per acre to plant as well as ratoon sugarcane crop   Soil Testing:  Conduct soil testing before planting to determine actual nutrient requirements.   Regional Variations:  Consult local agricultural universities or extension services for region-specific recommendations, as black cotton soils, red soils, and alluvial soils have different nutrient-holding capacities and requirements.   Proper fertiliser management at planting establishes a strong foundation for the sugarcane crop, promoting vigorous tillering, healthy root development, and ultimately higher cane yield with improved sugar recovery.   For further guidance or clarification, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

generated_question: My wheat plants are falling over, will that affect how much grain I get?
generated_answer: Yes, if your wheat plants are falling over, it can definitely hurt your yield. When the plants bend or fall, they get less sunlight and don't make as much food. This makes the grains not fill properly. In India, lodging can cut wheat yields by 12 to 66 percent. If it happens when the grains are forming, the loss can be as high as 80 percent. It also makes harvesting harder and can lead to more disease.
seed_question: The farmer wants to know whether there could be any reduction in the yield due lodging of wheat crop?
seed_answer: Lodging in wheat definitely reduces yield, and the loss can be serious depending on the stage and severity. When the crop bends or falls due to wind, rain, weak stems, excess nitrogen, or tall varieties, the plants receive less sunlight, photosynthesis drops, grains do not fill properly, and harvesting becomes difficult. Studies show that in India, lodging can reduce wheat yield by 12–66%, and in severe cases—when the crop falls flat during flowering or early grain filling—the loss can reach up to 80%. Lodging also increases disease risk and reduces grain quality. Therefore, yes, lodging leads to a clear reduction in wheat yield, especially when it occurs early in the grain-filling stage.

seed_question: What is the recommended application rate and method for manganese sulphate in wheat crop?
seed_answer: Manganese sulphate is an essential micronutrient fertilizer used to correct manganese deficiency in wheat crops.   Understanding Manganese Deficiency in Wheat: Symptoms of manganese deficiency begin to appear during the early growth stages of wheat and during the emergence of ears. Brownish - yellow stripes develop on the leaves, starting from the leaf tip and extending downward ; plant growth is reduced, and ears emerge late and twisted.  Manganese deficiency is increasing in rice wheat systems grown in Indo-gangetic plains having highly permeable coarse textured alkaline soils with low organic matter. The principle of any micronutrient including Mn recommendations for different crops and cropping systems is based on its available status in soil, requirements to meet the crops/cropping system and fixation in soil complex and other losses. Durum wheat genotypes are more susceptible to Mn deficiency than aestivum wheat. Manganese (Mn) can be supplied through the sulphate salt of Mn i.e. MnSO4 (Manganese sulphate) either through soil or foliar.  When symptoms of manganese deficiency appear in standing crops, apply 3 to 4 sprays of a 0.5 percent manganese sulfate solution at 10-15 day intervals . The first spray should be before the first irrigation, and the next 2-3 sprays should be after the first irrigation. Mix one kilogram of manganese sulfate in 200 liters of water and spray per acre. generated_question: My cotton has dark spots on the lint, what's causing that and how can I stop it?
generated_answer: The dark spots on your cotton lint are caused by honeydew from pests like aphids, whiteflies, and mealybugs. This sticky substance helps mold grow, which makes the lint look black. Keep your field clean, avoid too much nitrogen, and harvest when the cotton is dry. Always wear protection when using sprays.
seed_question: What causes blackening or darkening of cotton lint/fiber and how to prevent it?
seed_answer: Blackening or darkening of cotton lint/fiber is a significant quality deterioration issue that affects the marketability and commercial value of cotton produce. This problem, often referred to as 'black cotton' or 'sticky cotton,' can occur due to several factors including microbial contamination, insect honeydew deposits, and improper harvesting practices.   Primary Causes of Cotton Fiber Blackness: The main cause of blackening in cotton fiber is the contamination by honeydew secretions from sucking pests, particularly aphids, whiteflies, and mealybugs. These insects feed on cotton plant sap and excrete a sugary substance called honeydew on the cotton bolls and developing fiber. This honeydew provides an ideal substrate for the growth of sooty mold fungi (primarily Cladosporium species), which appear as black or dark gray deposits on the cotton lint. Additionally, bacterial and fungal infections during boll development, especially during periods of high humidity and rainfall, can cause direct blackening of the fiber.   Other contributing factors include late-season boll opening during rainy weather, poor field hygiene, delayed harvesting, and contact with contaminated soil or plant debris. Physiological disorders due to nutrient imbalances or water stress can also predispose cotton bolls to microbial colonization and subsequent blackening.   Symptoms and Characteristics: Affected cotton shows visible black or dark brown staining on the lint surface. The fiber may appear sticky due to honeydew deposits, making it difficult to process in ginning mills. Contaminated cotton has reduced spinning quality, lower fiber strength, and significantly diminished market value. The blackening can range from light spotting to severe discoloration covering large portions of the lint.   Integrated Management Practices: Preventive Cultural Practices: Maintain proper field sanitation by removing and destroying crop residues after harvest. Follow recommended spacing to ensure good air circulation and reduce humidity within the crop canopy. Avoid excessive nitrogen application, which promotes lush vegetative growth and attracts sucking pests. Practice timely irrigation to prevent water stress while avoiding over-irrigation that creates humid conditions favorable for fungal growth. Harvest cotton promptly when bolls are fully mature and dry, preferably during dry weather conditions.   Pest Management (Primary Strategy): Effective control of sucking pests is crucial to prevent honeydew accumulation. Monitor fields regularly for aphids, whiteflies, and mealybugs. For aphid control, spray Imidacloprid 17.8% SL at 40 ml per acre or Thiamethoxam 25% WG at 40 gm mixed in 120 to 150 liters of water. For whitefly management, apply Dimethoate 30% EC @ 300 ml or Oxydemeton Methyl 25% EC @ 300 ml or 1 litre Neem based insecticide alternately in 250 litres of water per acre of crop. For mealy bug, dissolve 1.5 gm Thiodicarb 75% WP or 4 ml Quinalphos 25% EC in one litre of water and spray it only on the affected and surrounding plants and if the pest spreads to the entire field, spray it two to three times in the entire field. If there is a severe infestation of mealybugs on the crop later, then use these insecticides again on the entire crop at an interval of 5 to 6 days.   Safety Precautions: When applying any pesticide, always wear protective clothing including long-sleeved shirts, long pants, gloves, and face masks. Avoid spraying during windy conditions or peak sunshine hours. Observe the Pre-Harvest Interval for insecticides. Store pesticides in original containers in a cool, dry place away from food items and out of children's reach. Follow label instructions carefully regarding dosage and application methods.   Additional Recommendations: Use resistant or tolerant cotton varieties when available. Maintain balanced fertilization with emphasis on potassium and micronutrients to improve plant health and natural resistance. Consider biological control agents like Beauveria bassiana or Lecanicillium lecanii for sucking pest management in organic production systems.   For further guidance or clarification regarding cotton fiber blackening issues, specific pest identification, or customized management recommendations for your local conditions, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

For example, you can use 24.3 to 30.4 kg of nitrogen, 24.3 kg of phosphorus, and 8.1 kg of potassium per acre. Also, add 10 kg of zinc sulphate per acre to prevent zinc deficiency. This helps the plants grow better and produce more grains.
seed_question: What is the recommended basal management practice for paddy cultivation?
seed_answer: Basal management in paddy cultivation is a critical practice that involves the application of essential nutrients at the time of land preparation or transplanting to ensure optimal crop establishment and growth. Proper basal fertilization sets the foundation for healthy plant development and maximum yield potential.    Soil Testing and Assessment:  Before applying any fertilizers, farmers should conduct a soil test to determine the existing nutrient status of their fields. This helps in making informed decisions about the type and quantity of nutrients required. Soil testing laboratories at Krishi Vigyan Kendras (KVKs) or agricultural universities can provide detailed soil health cards indicating pH levels, organic carbon content, and available NPK status.    Organic Matter Application: Apply 5t /acre of FYM or compost or green leaf manure @ 2.5 t/acre, during the last ploughing enriches soil organic matter, improves soil structure, and provides slow-release nutrients.  Apply 10 kg urea/acre at the time of first puddling while incorporating the stubbles of previous crop to compensate for the immobilisation of N by the stubbles. This may be done at least 10 days prior to planting of the subsequent crop.    NPK Fertilizer Management:  For basal application in paddy, apply 50% of the recommended nitrogen dose, the entire phosphorus requirement, and 50% of the potassium dose at the time of transplanting or within 2-3 days after transplanting. The typical recommendation for paddy is 50-60 kg Nitrogen per acre(50%to be applied at transplanting 24.3 – 30.4 kg N/acre), 60 kg Phosphorus (P2O5) per hectare(24.3 kg P₂O₅/acre), and 40 kg Potassium (K2O) per hectare(16.2 kg K₂O/acre, 50% to be applied at transplanting: 8.1 kg K₂O/acre), though this varies based on soil test results. Use fertilizers such as Urea for nitrogen, Single Super Phosphate or Di-ammonium Phosphate (DAP) for phosphorus, and Muriate of Potash for potassium.    Zinc Sulphate Application:  Zinc deficiency is common in paddy-growing areas, especially in alkaline and calcareous soils. As a basal application, incorporate Zinc Sulphate (21% Zn) at the rate of 10 kg per acre or Zinc Sulphate Heptahydrate at 2 kg per acre mixed with soil or sand for uniform distribution. This application significantly improves tillering, grain formation, and overall yield. Zinc application is particularly crucial in areas where paddy is grown continuously or in soils with high pH.   For further guidance or clarification, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

seed_question: How to grow Tomato in 1 hectare of land in Punjab?
seed_answer: Tomato is an important vegetable crop in Punjab, cultivated successfully under open field and protected conditions.Climate and Soil Tomato is a warm season crop and requires a relatively long growing season with plenty of sunshine and moderate day temperature of 20–280 C. It is sensitive to frost. Under low temperature, the plant growth is restricted and fruit setting is low. The critical factor in the setting of fruits is the night temperature, the optimum range being 15–200 C.The red pigment in the fruit will develop only when the temperature is between 150 C-300 C. Above this range of temperature, only the yellow pigment formed. When the temperature exceeds 400 C, no pigment will be formed. Tomato can be grown in all types of soils, but the soil should be friable. However, it grows best in light soils ranging from sandy loam to loam. Sowing time :  For winter planting, sowing should be done in October and transplanting in November-December.  economically covered with polythene bags of 35 ×25 cm size and of 100- gauge thickness. For February planting, sow seed in the end of November and protect seedlings from frost in the nursery beds by covering with polythene sheets or sarkanda thatch. However, the yield obtained would be comparatively lower from February planted crop than from November planted crop. To ensure successful growing of healthy seedlings from costly hybrid seeds of tomato, grow nursery of this crop under polyhouse (size 24′×13′×6′) made of UV stabilized low density polyethylene microns (800 guage) thickness. Nursery prepare:- Prepare 1.5 m wide and 20 cm high beds in an area of about two marlas (50 m2) to raise seedlings for an acre. Mix 5 quintals of well rotten farmyard manure with the soil and water the beds at least 10 days before sowing Manuare:- Manures and Fertilizers : Apply 10 tonnes of well rotten farmyard manure and plough it into the soil. Add 25 kg of N (55 kg of Urea) along with 25 kg of  P2 O5 (155 kg of Single Superphosphate) and 25 kg of K2 O (45 kg of Muriate of Potash) per acre in a band at 15 cm on one side of the bed mark and prepare the channels. After removing sarkanda/polythene bag cover by mid February, apply 35 kg of N (75kg of Urea) per acre in the rows, but not touching the base of the vines. Mix it with the soil and earth up. irrigation:- 1. Furrow irrigation: First irrigation should be given immediately after transplanting. Subsequent irrigations may be given after 6-7 days during summer and 10-15 days during winter months. Total number of irrigations required is 14 to 15.  2. Drip irrigation, fertigation and plastic mulching: Drip irrigation, fertigation and silver-black plastic mulching in rainy Varieties:- 1.Punjab Ratta’ are determinate with dense & dark green foliage. The fruits are medium sized, oval, very firm suitable for processing due to deep red colour with average lycopene content of 8 mg/100 g and TSS contents of 5%,yields 220-230 q/acre while the February transplanting gives 170-180 q/acre 2.Punjab Varkha Bahar-4, First picking  is  possible 88 days after transplanting. It is suitable for cultivation in rainy/autumn season. It is resistant to leaf curl virus. Its TSS is 3.8% and  lycopene content is 3.13 mg/100g of fresh weight.  Average yield is 245 q/acre. 3.Punjab Swarna (2018),. First picking is possible 120 days after transplanting. It gives early average yield (harvested till end March) of 166 q/acre and average yield of 1087 q/acre. It is suitable for table purpose.

seed_question: What are the IPM practices should I follow to control Green Leaf Hoppers?
seed_answer: Green Leaf Hopper (GLH), also known as green rice leafhopper (Nephotettix virescens), is a significant pest of paddy that causes both direct feeding damage and serves as a vector for viral diseases like tungro. Understanding and managing this pest is crucial for maintaining healthy paddy crops.   Pest Identification and Damage Symptoms: Green Leaf Hoppers are small, wedge-shaped insects, approximately 3-4 mm long, with a characteristic green coloration. They feed by sucking sap from rice leaves, causing yellowing and drying of leaf tips (a condition called 'hopper burn'). Heavy infestations lead to stunted growth, reduced tillering, and significant yield losses. More importantly, GLH transmits Rice Tungro Virus, which causes severe economic damage to paddy crops.   Severity Assessment: Monitor your paddy fields regularly, especially 15-45 days after transplanting. Check the undersides of leaves during early morning or late evening when hoppers are most active. Initial infestations show scattered yellowing, while severe infestations display widespread leaf discoloration and plant stunting.   Control Measures:  The most effective means of GLH management is to use GLH-resistant and tungro-resistant varieties, such as IR 50, IR 54, IR 64, CR 1009, PY 3, Co 46, and White Ponni.  For Severe Infestations: Chemical control is recommended when pest population exceeds ETL level. The economic threshold level (ETL) of brown planthopper is 60 insects per 25 net sweeps, or 5 insects per hill at the vegetative stage, 10 insects per hill at the flowering stage, and 2 insects per hill in tungro-endemic areas. If ETL exceeds, spray or broadcast any one of the following insecticides twice at 15 and 30 days after transplanting per acre: Imidacloprid 17.8% SL @ 40-50 ml per acre, thiamethoxam 25% WG @ 40 g per acre, carbofuran 3% CG @ 10 kg per acre, buprofezin 25% SC @ 320 ml per acre, fipronil 5% SC @ 400- 600 ml per acre, fipronil 0.3% G @ 6.7- 10 kg per acre. Use 200-300 liters of water volume for one acre spray.  Biological control: The most abundant predator is the green mired bug Cyrtorhinus lividipennis, which predate on both eggs and nymphs.   Cultural Practices:  - Remove alternate hosts and weeds from field bunds  - Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization, which attracts hoppers  - Maintain proper plant spacing for good air circulation  - Use light traps to monitor and reduce adult populations - Intercrop soybean in upland rice to reduce the incidence of leafhoppers in rice.  Trap Methods: - Set up light traps to attract and kill the moths.  - Set up light trap one for at least 1 acre. - Set up bird perches (16-18 /acre) of Insectivorous Bird sat vegetative phase of crop. - Monitor through pheromone traps (@ 4-5/acre for timely control measures. Change the lure at 15-20 days intervals   For further guidance or clarification, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

generated_question: My maize plants are being attacked by worms and weeds. What can I do?
generated_answer: You need to control armyworm, silk cutter, and motha weed in your maize. For armyworm, spray chlorantraniliprole or emamectin benzoate as directed. For older crops, you can apply a mixture of insecticide and soil in the whorls. For silk cutter, spray chlorantraniliprole 2–3 weeks after sowing. seed_question: How to control armyworm, silk cutter, and motha (purple nutsedge) weed in maize crop?
seed_answer: This query addresses integrated pest and weed management in maize, specifically targeting armyworm, silk cutter (corn earworm), and motha weed (purple nutsedge - Cyperus rotundus). Let me address the pest management first, followed by weed control.   1-Fall armyworm: The young larvae feed by scraping the leaf surface, making papery windows. The bigger larvae feed voraciously on the central whorl leaves, causing round to oblong holes and producing a large amount of faecal matter. The larvae can be identified by a predominant white-coloured inverted Y-shaped mark on the head and the presence of four spots arranged in a square pattern at the tail end.  Adopt the following control measures: • Sow the crop at recommended time only.  • Avoid staggered sowing of maize in adjacent fields to minimize spread of this pest. • Regularly monitor the field to collect and destroy egg masses of fall armyworm from  leaves. Egg masses are covered with hairs and are easily visible. • Spray the crop with chlorantraniliprole 18.5 SC @ 0.4 mL per litre of water or emamectin benzoate 5 SG @ 0.4 g per litre of water using 120 litres of water per acre for crop up to 20 days old. For older crop, increase the spray volume to 200 litres of water per acre with corresponding increase in dosage of the above insecticides. For effective management, direct the nozzle towards the whorl.  • If the infestation is in patches or the crop is more than 40 days old and spraying is difficult, apply soil–insecticide/biopesticide mixture (about 0.5 g) in the whorls of infested plants. To prepare the mixture, add 5 mL chlorantraniliprole 18.5 SC or 5 g emamectin benzoate 5 SG in 10 mL water and mix well in 1 kg of soil, then apply in the whorl of infested plants.  2-Armyworm and silk cutter: These insects feed on the leaves in the whorl of maize plants, causing damage to the foliage and reducing plant vigour. Spray the crop 2–3 weeks after sowing, as soon as injury to the leaves is noticed, with chlorantraniliprole 18.5 SC @ 30 mL per acre using 60 litres of water per acre with a knapsack sprayer. Please contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) for proper pest identification before taking any management action, as fall armyworm, silk cutter and armyworm are important pests of maize. Correct pest identification helps in better management and avoids unnecessary crop input and pesticide use.  3- MOTHA (PURPLE NUTSEDGE) WEED CONTROL: Motha (Cyperus rotundus) is a perennial sedge weed with extensive underground tuber system, making it difficult to control.  Cultural method: Give two hoeings at about 15 to 30 days after sowing using khurpa, kasaula, wheel-hoe, triphali or tractor-drawn cultivator to control weeds. Alternatively, uniform spreading of 30 quintals of paddy straw mulch per acre at the time of sowing provides effective control of annual weeds. Another option is to grow one or two rows of cowpea between maize rows and harvest it as fodder 35 to 45 days after sowing; after that, no additional weed control operation is required. For intercropping of cowpea, use 8 kg seed per acre of variety CL 367. It does not require any additional fertilizer. Sow maize and cowpea simultaneously.  Spray tembotrione 34.4% SC @ 105 mL per acre in 150 litres of water at 20 days after sowing for effective control of mixed weed flora. For the control of dila/motha, apply 2,4-D amine salt 58 SL @ 400 mL per acre as post-emergence at 20–25 days after sowing in 150 litres of water.  For further guidance on pest identification, Economic Threshold Level determination, selection of appropriate pesticides, resistant varieties, or clarification on integrated management practices, farmers are strongly advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer in Tarn Taran district. KVK experts can provide field-specific recommendations based on pest intensity and crop stage.

generated_answer: If armyworms are eating your maize leaves, spray chlorantraniliprole or emamectin benzoate as instructed. For motha weeds, hoe the field twice or use paddy straw mulch. You can also grow cowpea between the maize rows. Spray tembotrione or 2,4-D amine salt to control the weeds.

generated_question: My wheat plants are getting yellow leaves, what's causing it and how can I fix it?
generated_answer: Yellow leaves in wheat, also called 'panda,' are usually because the soil lacks zinc, iron, or nitrogen. This happens a lot in areas like Kaushambi in Uttar Pradesh, where the soil is alkaline and doesn’t release nutrients properly. If the leaves are yellow with green veins, it's likely zinc deficiency. If the new leaves are uniformly yellow, it might be iron. If older leaves are pale, it's probably nitrogen. To fix it, spray 2 kg zinc sulphate and 6 kg urea in 320 liters of water per acre. If you already used urea, mix 1 kg zinc sulphate with 1 kg lime water instead. Apply 10 kg zinc sulphate per acre with farmyard manure before sowing. Make sure to use the right fertilizers and keep the soil pH balanced. If the yellowing doesn’t go away, check for other problems like disease or water issues.
seed_question: How to manage yellow leaves (chlorosis/panda) in wheat crop?
seed_answer: Yellow leaves or 'panda' in wheat is a common nutrient deficiency symptom, primarily caused by lack of zinc, iron, or nitrogen in the soil. In Uttar Pradesh, particularly in districts like Kaushambi, alkaline and calcareous soils often lock up micronutrients, making them unavailable to plants. This deficiency typically appears during the tillering to jointing stage of wheat and can significantly reduce yields if not addressed promptly.   Symptom Identification: Yellowing (chlorosis) in wheat manifests differently based on the deficient nutrient. Zinc deficiency shows interveinal chlorosis on younger leaves, with green veins and yellow tissue between them. Leaves may develop brown necrotic spots and plants appear stunted with reduced tillering. Iron deficiency causes uniform yellowing of young leaves while older leaves remain green. Nitrogen deficiency shows uniform paleyellow color starting from older leaves and progressing upward, with overall weak plant growth.  Corrective Management: If the symptom of deficiency of zinc appears in standing crop, spray 2 kg zinc sulphate and 6 kg urea dissolved in 320 liter water per acre. If the top dressing of urea is already done, spray zinc sulphate dissolved in 1 kg lime water in place of urea (dissolve 1 kg quick lime in 4 liter water in the evening and filter it in the morning and use filtered water and through away the lime).Please note that it is essential to use urea or lime water with zinc sulphate. If zinc sulphate has not been used in paddy field as basal dressing and there is possibility of deficiency, top dress with 8-10 kg zinc sulphate.    Soil Application for Long-term Management: For sustained correction and prevention in subsequent crops, apply 10 kg Zinc Sulphate per acre mixed with soil or farmyard manure before sowing. In severely deficient soils, combine this with 10 kg Ferrous Sulphate per acre. Soil application is more economical and provides lasting benefits for 2-3 crop cycles.   Integrated Nutrient Management: Ensure balanced fertilization with recommended NPK doses for wheat in UP (48 kg Nitrogen, 24 kg Phosphorus, 16 kg Potassium per acre). Split nitrogen application - 50% at sowing, 25% at first irrigation (CRI stage, 21 days), and 25% at second irrigation helps maintain green foliage. Incorporate well-decomposed farmyard manure (4-4.8 tonnes/acre) to improve soil structure and micronutrient availability.   Preventive Measures:  - Test soil regularly (every 2-3 years) to identify nutrient deficiencies  - Treat wheat seeds with zinc-enriched solutions before sowing  - Avoid excessive use of phosphatic fertilizers which can induce zinc deficiency  - Maintain proper soil pH (6.5-7.5) through judicious use of amendments  - Ensure adequate irrigation but avoid waterlogging which reduces nutrient uptake   Precautions During Spraying: Wear protective clothing, gloves, and face mask while preparing and applying spray solution. Do not eat, drink, or smoke during application. Wash hands and exposed body parts thoroughly after spraying. Keep children and livestock away from freshly sprayed fields for at least 24 hours.   Important Note: If yellowing persists despite corrective measures, it could indicate other issues like disease infection, waterlogging, or salinity stress. For further guidance and soil testing facilities, please contact your nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) in Kaushambi district or the District Agriculture Office.

generated_question: I'm growing wheat in Saharanpur, and I'm not sure what to do for the nutrients. Can you help?
generated_answer: For wheat in Saharanpur, you need to use a mix of nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium. If you plant on time, use 48 kg nitrogen, 24 kg P₂O₅, and 16 kg K₂O per acre. That means 104 kg urea, 150 kg SSP, and 27 kg MOP. If you plant late, use less—32 kg nitrogen, 16 kg P₂O₅, and 12 kg K₂O. How you apply the nutrients depends on your soil. generated_question: My Berseem crop is getting damaged by some pests, and I don't know what they are. What can I do?
generated_answer: The pests you're seeing are called Bihar hairy caterpillars, also known as Babu Kuta. They attack Berseem twice a season, once in September-October and again in March-April. They eat the plants from the bottom up, and you might need to resow. Weeds like Gutpatna, Bathu, Jangli Palak, and wild castor can host these pests. Remove those weeds near your field before planting. Keeping the area clean and encouraging natural predators can help control them. These are the main steps to manage the problem.
seed_question: What are the effective control measures for Babu Kuta (whitefly) infestation in Berseem crop?
seed_answer: "Babu Kuta" (or Pabbu Kutta) is a regional term used in Punjabi for Bihar Hairy Caterpillars. In the context of Berseem (Egyptian Clover), these pests are often identified as:   Bihar hairy caterpillar (Babu Kuta): It is polyphagous and sporadic pest. It attacks berseem crop twice in a season. In September-October larvae devour the young crop to the ground level and the farmers have to resow it. Again, it feeds voraciously on plant foliage and causes severe losses in March-April. Weeds like Gutpatna, Bathu, Jangli Palak and wild castor are the alternate host plants on which the pest feeds in gregarious phase and later on migrates to berseem. Destroy these weeds growing adjoining to berseem fields before its sowing. which creates favourable conditions for pest multiplication and reduces the activity of predatory birds that naturally control caterpillars. Maintaining clean field surroundings and encouraging natural enemies are important preventive measures. These cultural and ecological practices form the primary IPM strategy for managing Bihar hairy caterpillar in berseem crops.  For further guidance or clarification, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

seed_question: What is the recommended application interval between inorganic chemical fertilizers and organic fertilizers in paddy cultivation?
seed_answer: The recommended application interval between inorganic chemical fertilizers and organic fertilizers in paddy cultivation is generally 10 days. This interval is crucial for optimizing nutrient uptake, preventing nutrient interactions and ensuring the effectiveness of both organic and inorganic inputs.   Understanding the Application Interval :   When both organic and inorganic fertilizers are used in paddy fields, proper timing is essential. Organic fertilizers such as farmyard manure (FYM), vermicompost or green manure work differently than chemical fertilizers. Organic inputs release nutrients slowly through microbial decomposition, while inorganic fertilizers provide readily available nutrients. Applying them simultaneously can lead to nutrient fixation, reduced microbial activity and decreased efficiency of organic amendments. The 10-day interval allows organic materials to begin decomposition and establish beneficial microbial populations before chemical fertilizers are applied.   Soil Testing and Nutrient Requirements :   Before applying any fertilizers, farmers should conduct soil testing to determine the exact nutrient status of their paddy fields. generated_question: How long does it take for fodder maize to be ready for cutting, and when is the best time to do it?
generated_answer: Fodder maize usually takes 65 to 90 days from sowing to be ready for cutting. It's best to harvest when the plants are in the milk ripe stage or the dough stage. Some varieties like J 1008 take about 73 days to reach the milking stage, while others take longer. Don't remove the cobs when cutting, as they add nutrition. You can also preserve extra fodder as silage.
seed_question: What is the maturity period for fodder maize varieties and when should I harvest for optimal forage quality?
seed_answer: The maturity period for fodder maize is a critical factor that directly influences both the quantity and quality of forage yield. Unlike grain maize that is harvested when kernels reach physiological maturity, fodder maize has different maturity considerations focused on optimal biomass production and nutritional value for livestock.  Maturity Period of Fodder Maize Varieties:  Fodder maize varieties typically have a growth duration ranging from 65 to 90 days from sowing to the optimal harvesting stage. It takes about 50 to 60 days to become available for harvesting. The fodder is considered good for milch animals.  The maturity period varies based on the variety selected: J 1008 (2024) is the first yellow-seeded, early maturing composite maize variety, which reaches the milking stage in 73 days about 12 days earlier than J 1006 (86 days) and J 1007 (84 days). It reaches in dough stage about 10 days from milking stage.   Harvesting: Harvest the crop when the plants are between the milk ripe stage and the dough stage of grain development. From silking onwards, the nutritive value of the plant is mainly in the grains, hence do not remove cobs from that fodder crop. Preserve surplus fodder maize as silage  For specific variety recommendations, optimal sowing times, and harvesting schedules tailored to your agro-climatic zone, consult your nearest Agricultural Extension Officer or Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK). They can provide location-specific guidance on the best fodder maize varieties and management practices for your farm.

generated_question: I’m growing maize, and I want to know when and how to apply herbicides properly?
generated_answer: The herbicide Stomp isn’t recommended for maize. Instead, you can do two hoeings with tools like khurpa or a tractor-drawn cultivator between 15 to 30 days after sowing. You can also spread 30 quintals of paddy straw mulch per acre to control weeds. Another option is to intercrop with cowpea using 8 kg of seed per acre. For chemical control, you can spray Atrazine or Tembotrione at specific rates depending on soil type and weed stage. Other options like 2,4-D or Paraquat can also be used as directed. Combining these methods helps manage weeds well.
seed_question: What is the appropriate timing and method for applying Stomp herbicide in maize?
seed_answer: The product you mentioned contains the active ingredient pendimethalin 30% EC, which is not recommended for use in maize as per the Package of Practices and PPQ guidelines. Therefore, you should adopt other recommended weed management practices to effectively control weeds in maize. For cultural management, do two hoeings at 15–30 days after sowing using khurpa, kasaula, wheel-hoe, triphali or a tractor-drawn cultivator; spreading about 30 quintals of paddy straw mulch per acre at sowing suppresses annual weeds, or intercrop maize with cowpea by sowing 1–2 rows between maize rows using 8 kg seed per acre (variety CL 367) and harvest it as fodder 35–45 days after sowing, which naturally suppresses weeds without additional fertilizer.  For chemical weed control, spray Atrazine 50% WP @ 800 g/acre on medium to heavy soils or 500 g/acre on light soils within 10 days after sowing using 200 litres of water, or apply Atrazine @ 250 g/acre as a 20 cm band over crop rows followed by hoeing at 15–30 days after sowing to control annual grasses and broadleaf weeds, especially Trianthema; alternatively spray Tembotrione 34.4% SC @ 105 ml/acre in 150 litres of water at about 20 days after sowing for mixed weeds, and for motha (Cyperus spp.) apply 2,4-D amine salt 58% SL @ 400 ml/acre as post-emergence at 20–25 days after sowing in 150 litres of water.  Other herbicide options include 2,4-D Ethyl ester 38% EC @ about 1 L/acre mixed in 160–180 L of water/acre, which controls broad-leaf weeds and some sedges such as Trianthema monogyna, Amaranthus spp., Portulaca oleracea, Tribulus terrestris, Boerhavia diffusa, Euphorbia hirta and Cyperus spp. with a 50–60 day pre-harvest interval; or Paraquat Dichloride 24% SL @ 324–810 ml/acre can be used as a pre-plant spray before sowing or as directed inter-row application at the 2–3 leaf stage of weeds to control broad-leaf weeds, grasses and sedges like Cyperus rotundus, Commelina benghalensis, Trianthema monogyna, Amaranthus spp. and Echinochloa spp.; another option is Diuron 80% WP @ about 400 g/acre mixed in 240 litres of water/acre followed by one manual weeding, which controls grassy weeds, broad-leaf weeds and sedges such as Cyperus iria, Echinochloa spp., Digitaria spp., Chenopodium album, Eleusine spp., Amaranthus spp. and Phyllanthus niruri. By combining timely sowing, proper spacing, interculture operations and recommended herbicides, you can effectively manage weeds in maize, reduce crop–weed competition, and achieve better crop growth and higher yield.

generated_question: I've heard about drones for farming. Is there any help to buy one?
generated_answer: Yes, the government offers help to buy agricultural drones. You can get up to 100% of the cost, but not more than 10 lakh rupees, if you're an ICAR institute, KVK, or a government institution. Agricultural graduates can get up to 5 lakh rupees or 50% of the cost, whichever is less. FPOs and cooperatives get up to 4 lakh rupees or 40% of the cost. You need to apply through your state's agriculture department website. Contact your local agriculture office for help with the process.
seed_question: Information about the Subsidy of Agricultural Drone..?
seed_answer: The Government of India, under the Sub-Mission on Agricultural Mechanization scheme, provides subsidies for purchasing agricultural drones and their auxiliary equipment to promote modern farming practices and efficient input application. For the promotion of Kisan Drones, the following provisions have been made under the guidelines of the Sub-Mission on Agricultural Mechanization (SMAM).  The scheme identifies these categories of beneficiaries: - Small & Marginal, SC/ST, Women, NE states farmers, FPOs, Cooperative societies, Rural entrepreneurs / CHCs, Agriculture graduates (as a category)    Eligibility Criteria for Agricultural Graduates: Agriculture graduates establishing Custom Hiring Centres are eligible. This ensures that beneficiaries have established agricultural service provision experience.   Subsidy Amounts: Financial assistance @100% of the cost of a drone, up to a maximum of ₹10 lakh per drone, is provided to ICAR institutes, KVKs, SAUs and other Government institutions for demonstration purposes For Agricultural Graduates (Agri Junction operators): Maximum subsidy of 5 lakh or 50% of the drone cost, whichever is lower. For FPOs and Cooperative Societies: Maximum subsidy of 4 lakh or 40% of the drone cost, whichever is lower.   Application Process: Interested beneficiaries (FPOs, cooperative societies and agricultural graduates) must register on the official Agriculture Department website (www.upagriculture.com for Uttar Pradesh; respective state agriculture department websites for other states). Selection is done on a "first-come, first-served" basis, so early registration is advantageous.   Application Procedure: Farmers should contact their District Deputy Director of Agriculture, Block Seed Store In-charge or Block Technology Manager (BTM) for detailed application procedures, required documents, and scheme guidelines specific to their region.  For further guidance or clarification, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

generated_question: My pea plants are not growing well, and the roots look bad. What's going on?
generated_answer: The problem might be root-knot nematodes. They attack the roots and cause stunting, yellowing leaves, and root galls. The roots may look brown and water-soaked. To control this, plough deeply in summer, use solarization with plastic sheets, and plant resistant varieties. Don't sow late, and check the soil before planting. Keep the field clean and avoid spreading infected soil.
seed_question: What are the effective control measures for root knot nematodes in peas (matar)?
seed_answer: Root-knot Nematode feeds on the tender roots and base of the pseudostem, causing stunting, chlorosis, poor tillering, and necrosis of leaves as common above-ground symptoms. Characteristic root galls and lesions are seen on the roots, which may lead to rotting. The infected roots often show brown, water-soaked areas in the outer tissues, and nematode infestation can further aggravate root rot disease.  -Survival and spread: Nematodes survive in the soil and infected roots as primary inoculum. Plant residues from infected crops remaining in the field act as a reservoir, and the pest spreads through infected plants and soil.  -Favourable conditions: Warm and moist soil conditions favour the development and multiplication of nematodes.  Cultural control: -Deep summer ploughing helps control nematodes and also exposes dormant stages of insect pests (pupa and larva), reducing their initial population. -Soil solarization: Cover beds with a transparent polythene sheet of about 100-gauge thickness for three weeks before sowing to reduce soil-borne pests and weeds. -Use resistant or tolerant varieties where available. -Avoid late sowing of the crop to reduce infestation risk.   Monitoring and Prevention: Regular soil testing for nematode populations before planting helps in decision-making. Maintain field hygiene and avoid movement of infested soil through farm equipment, water, or planting material. Early detection and prompt action are crucial for effective management.   For further guidance or clarification, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

generated_question: Matar ki akali mein nematode hota hai, kya kya tareeqe kaam karte hain?
generated_answer: Nematode akali ke akali aur nihayat me akali ke nishan dekhe jate hain. Unhe control karne ke liye, khudai ke samay ghumakar kharap karna chahiye. Bhi 100-gauge ki transparent polythene sheet laga ke 3 hafta tak khudai karna chahiye. Kisi bhi varieti ko chuna jisme nematode ke khilaaf kamaal hota ho. Aakhri sowing na karein taki infestation kam ho. Aapko pahle soil ke test karna chahiye taki samay par kuch karo.

generated_answer: You can control root knot nematodes by plowing deeply during summer to reduce their population. Cover the beds with a transparent polythene sheet for three weeks before planting to kill pests. Use resistant pea varieties if possible. Avoid planting too late to reduce infestation. Keep the field clean and test the soil for nematodes before sowing. Don't move infected soil around with equipment or water.
seed_question: What are the effective control measures for root knot nematodes in peas (matar)?
seed_answer: Root-knot Nematode feeds on the tender roots and base of the pseudostem, causing stunting, chlorosis, poor tillering, and necrosis of leaves as common above-ground symptoms. Characteristic root galls and lesions are seen on the roots, which may lead to rotting. The infected roots often show brown, water-soaked areas in the outer tissues, and nematode infestation can further aggravate root rot disease.  -Survival and spread: Nematodes survive in the soil and infected roots as primary inoculum. Plant residues from infected crops remaining in the field act as a reservoir, and the pest spreads through infected plants and soil.  -Favourable conditions: Warm and moist soil conditions favour the development and multiplication of nematodes.  Cultural control: -Deep summer ploughing helps control nematodes and also exposes dormant stages of insect pests (pupa and larva), reducing their initial population. -Soil solarization: Cover beds with a transparent polythene sheet of about 100-gauge thickness for three weeks before sowing to reduce soil-borne pests and weeds. -Use resistant or tolerant varieties where available. -Avoid late sowing of the crop to reduce infestation risk.   Monitoring and Prevention: Regular soil testing for nematode populations before planting helps in decision-making. Maintain field hygiene and avoid movement of infested soil through farm equipment, water, or planting material. Early detection and prompt action are crucial for effective management.   For further guidance or clarification, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

generated_question: How do I apply MAP foliar spray to my rice crop and what strength should I use?
generated_answer: You can apply MAP foliar spray to your rice crop at a concentration of 0.5-1%, which is 5-10 grams of MAP per litre of water. Apply it 30-40 days after sowing, up to the pre-flowering stage, 2-3 times with 10-15 days in between. Spray in the early morning or late evening when it's cooler. Use a knapsack sprayer with a fine nozzle and spray about 80-100 litres per acre. Make sure the leaves are fully wet but not dripping. Avoid spraying in the wind or when it's too hot. Wear gloves, a mask, and eye protection when mixing and applying. This helps the crop grow better and can increase yield.
seed_question: What is the recommended concentration and method for foliar application of MAP (Mono Ammonium Phosphate) in rice cultivation?
seed_answer: Foliar application of MAP (Mono Ammonium Phosphate) is an effective supplementary nutrition strategy for rice crops, particularly during critical growth stages when root uptake may be limited or when quick nutrient correction is needed.   Soil Testing and Nutrient Assessment: Before applying any foliar spray, conduct a soil test to understand the baseline phosphorus and nitrogen levels. This helps determine whether foliar supplementation is necessary alongside basal fertiliser applications. Foliar feeding should complement, not replace, soil-applied fertilisers.   Recommended Concentration and Application: For rice crops, the recommended concentration of MAP for foliar spray is 0.5-1% solution (5-10 g MAP per litre of water). This concentration ensures effective nutrient absorption through leaves without causing phytotoxicity or leaf burn. When applying fertilizer through Leafy Spray method Mono Ammonium Phosphate (12-61-0) should be used 30-40 days after sowing of the crop till pre-flowering stage for 2-3 times at the gap of 10-15 days.   Timing of Application: Apply MAP foliar spray during critical growth stages: tillering stage (20-30 days after transplanting), panicle initiation stage (45-50 days), and flowering stage (70-75 days). Early morning (6-9 AM) or late evening (4-6 PM) applications are ideal when stomata are open and temperatures are moderate, ensuring better nutrient absorption and minimal evaporation losses.   Application Method: Use a knapsack sprayer with a fine nozzle to ensure uniform coverage of both upper and lower leaf surfaces. Spray approximately 80-100 litres of solution per acre. Ensure complete wetting of leaves without causing excessive dripping. Avoid spraying during windy conditions, high temperatures, or when rain is expected within 6 hours.   Organic Alternatives: For organic farming systems, consider alternatives like vermiwash, panchagavya, or jeevamrut, which provide balanced nutrients naturally. These can be applied at 3-5% concentration as foliar sprays.   When handling and applying MAP fertilizer solution, wear chemical-resistant gloves to prevent skin irritation, use a face mask to avoid inhaling dust particles during preparation, wear protective clothing including long sleeves and full pants, and use eye protection (goggles) to prevent solution splashes. After application, wash your hands and face thoroughly with soap and water. Avoid eating, drinking, or smoking during preparation and application.  Expected Benefits: Proper foliar application of MAP enhances tillering, promotes healthy panicle development, improves grain filling, increases grain weight, and can boost yields by 8-12% when combined with optimal agronomic practices. Visible improvements typically appear within 7-10 days of application.   For further guidance or clarification, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

generated_question: I have sandy soil, what maize varieties can I grow?
generated_answer: There are no specific maize varieties mentioned for sandy soil. However, you can try PMH 14, PMH 13, or ADV 9293 for sandy-loam to silty-loam soils. These hybrids mature in about 97 to 98 days and give good yields. For shorter growing seasons, PMH 2 is a good option that matures in 83 days. Check with local experts for advice based on your soil and climate.
seed_question: Which maize varieties are suitable for cultivation in sandy soil conditions?
seed_answer: According to Punjab POP recommended soil type is sandy-loam to silty-loam soils. There is no mention of varieties suitable for sandy cultivation.  Recommended varieties for sandy-loam to silty-loam soils.  Long Duration Varieties/Hybrids - PMH 14 (2023): This single cross hybrid has tall plants with broad and erect leaves. Tassel is semi-open and medium. Ears are long, medium placed with yellow-orange flintcapped grains. It matures in 98 days and average grain yield is 24.8 quintals per acre. It is moderately resistant to fall army worm.  - PMH 13 (2021): This single cross hybrid has tall plants with medium high earplacement. Leaves are dark green and broad. Tassel is medium and open. Ears are conico-cylindrical and long with light orange flint grains. It matures in 97 days and average grain yield is 24.0 quintals per acre. It is moderately resistant to maydis leaf blight, charcoal rot and maize stem borer.  - ADV 9293 (2021): This single cross hybrid has tall plants with medium ear height. Stem is sturdy and leaves are broad. Tassel is medium and open. Ears are long with attractive orange flint grains. It matures in 97 days and average grain yield is 24.5 quintals per acre. It is moderately resistant to maydis leaf blight, charcoal rot and maize stem borer.  Medium Duration Variety: - JC 4 (2021): The plants of this composite variety are medium tall in height. Ears are medium placed and long. This composite has deep-orange and bold grains. It is tested ‘very good’ for chappati quality parameters viz, taste, texture, appearance and flavour. It matures in about 90 days. It yields about 13.0 quintals per acre. This variety is recommended for irrigated and kandi areas of the state. This variety is also recommended for organic farming. Short Duration Hybrid: - PMH 2 (2005): It is a short duration single cross hybrid. It has medium plant height with medium ear placement. Leaves are medium sized and dark green in colour. Tassel is of medium size and semi-open. Silk is of green colour. It has medium long ears. Grains are yellow orange flint with yellow caps. The hybrid resists lodging and is tolerant to bacterial stalk rot. It matures in about 83 days and average grain yield is 18.0 quintals per acre.  For further guidance on variety selection specific to your soil test results, local climatic conditions, and detailed cultivation practices tailored to sandy soils, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer at the Department of Agriculture, Rupnagar.

Stem is sturdy and leaves are broad. Tassel is medium and open. Ears are long with attractive orange flint grains. It matures in 97 days and average grain yield is 24.5 quintals per acre. Ears are medium placed and long. This composite has deep-orange and bold grains. It is tested ‘very good’ for chappati quality parameters viz, taste, texture, appearance and flavour. It matures in about 90 days. It yields about 13.0 quintals per acre. This variety is recommended for irrigated and kandi areas of the state. This variety is also recommended for organic farming. seed_question: What is the information regarding NPK 19-19-19 and Sagarika liquid fertilizer for wheat cultivation?
seed_answer: NPK 19-19-19 and Sagarika liquid fertilizer are water-soluble fertilizer products used for providing balanced nutrition to wheat crops. Understanding their composition, application methods, and timing is essential for achieving optimal crop performance in wheat cultivation.  NPK 19-19-19 Composition and Benefits: NPK 19-19-19 is a completely water-soluble fertilizer containing equal proportions of Nitrogen (N), Phosphorus (P), and Potassium (K) at 19% each. This balanced formulation provides: - Nitrogen for vegetative growth, tillering, and protein content in grains - Phosphorus for strong root development, grain formation, and early maturity - Potassium for disease resistance, water regulation, and grain quality  This fertilizer is particularly beneficial during critical growth stages when wheat plants require balanced nutrition for both vegetative and reproductive development.  Sagarika Liquid Fertilizer: Sagarika is a liquid seaweed-based biostimulant fertilizer derived from marine algae extracts. It contains natural plant growth hormones (auxins, cytokinins, gibberellins), micronutrients, amino acids, and organic compounds that: - Enhance nutrient uptake efficiency - Improve stress tolerance (drought, cold, heat) - Promote root development and tillering - Increase chlorophyll content for better photosynthesis - Improve grain filling and overall yield  Application Recommendations for Wheat:  NPK 19-19-19 Application  The recommended dose of fertilizer through the drip irrigation is 1.5-2 g per litre of water, considering the soil and crop. When applying through foliar spray, fertilizer should be used after 30-40 days after sowing of the crop till the flowering stage at 0.5 %-1.0 % (5-10 g per litre of water) 2-3 times with an interval of 10-15 days.  Sagarika Liquid Application: - Dosage: 5 ml per liter of water (500 ml per acre) - Application timing: - First spray: 15-20 days after sowing (CRI stage) - Second spray: 35-40 days after sowing (Active tillering) - Third spray: 90-95 days after sowing (Flowering stage) - Method: Foliar spray, ensuring complete coverage of leaves  Combination Strategy: Both fertilizers can be used together in an integrated nutrient management program: - Use NPK 19-19-19 for primary macronutrient supplementation - Use Sagarika as a biostimulant to enhance nutrient use efficiency - They can be tank-mixed for combined application, but conduct a small compatibility test first - Maintain pH of spray solution between 6.0 and 7.0 for optimal results  Advantages of Water-Soluble Fertilizers: - Quick nutrient availability to plants - Higher absorption efficiency through leaves - Reduced soil application and labor costs - Flexibility in application timing based on crop needs - Minimal nutrient losses compared to soil application  Precautions: - Avoid spraying during hot midday hours (10 AM - 4 PM) to prevent leaf burn - Do not mix with pesticides containing copper, sulfur, or strong alkaline products without compatibility testing - Use clean water free from high salt content - Store fertilizers in cool, dry places away from direct sunlight - Always dissolve completely before spraying to avoid nozzle clogging  Soil Testing Importance: Before applying any fertilizer, conduct soil testing to determine actual nutrient requirements. Water-soluble fertilizers should supplement, not replace, basal fertilizer application.  For further guidance on fertilizer application rates, timing specific to your soil conditions, and integrated nutrient management practices, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.
