generated_question: My fodder maize is just 10-15 days old, and I'm worried about insects. What can I do to protect it?
generated_answer: At this stage, insects like maize borer and fall armyworm can harm your crop. You can spray 40 mL of chlorantraniliprole 18.5 SC in 60-80 litres of water per acre. Don't feed the fodder for 21 days after spraying. You can also use tricho-cards with parasitized eggs. Place 50 strips in the crop during the evening. Make sure to follow safety rules and wear protective gear. Avoid feeding animals for 21 days after spraying.
seed_question: What insect control measures should be taken 10-15 days after sowing fodder maize to protect the young crop?
seed_answer: The critical period of 10-15 days after sowing is vital for protecting young fodder maize seedlings from early-stage insect pests that can severely compromise stand establishment and subsequent biomass yield. At this vulnerable growth stage, several damaging pests may attack, requiring timely intervention to ensure optimal forage production.   Maize borer  Attacks the crop from March to October.  For effective control, the attacked plants should be uprooted and destroyed after 2-3 weeks of sowing. -  It can also be controlled with spray of 40 mL chlorantraniliprole 18.5 SC in 60-80 litres of water per acre Do not feed the fodder for atleast 21 days after the spray of chlorantraniliprole 18.5 SC .  - Alternatively use tricho-cards twice having 50,000 eggs of Corcyra cephalonica per acre parasitized by Trichogramma chilonis; first release on 10 days old crop and second one week after first release. -  Cut tricho-cards into 50 strips, each having approximately 1000 parasitized eggs. Place these strips in the central whorl uniformly at 50 spots per acre during evening hours. These tricho-cards are available at the Biocontrol Labs, Department of Entomology, PAU Ludhiana and Regional Station, Gurdaspur.  Fall armyworm:  The young larvae feed by scrapping the leaf surface making papery windows. The bigger larvae feed voraciously on the central whorl leaves causing round to oblong holes and produce a large amount of faecal matter. The larva can be identified by predominant white-coloured inverted Y-shaped mark on the head and presence of four spots arranged in square pattern at the tail end.   Adopt the following control measures: • Avoid staggered sowing in adjacent fields. • Prefer mixed cropping of fodder maize with cowpea/ bajra/ sorghum. • Use recommended seed rate (30 kg per acre) and follow line sowing method rather than broadcasting. • Regularly monitor the field to collect and destroy egg masses of fall armyworm from leaves. Egg masses are covered with hairs and are easily visible. • Spray the crop with chlorantraniliprole 18.5 SC @ 0.4 mL per litre using120 litres of water per acre, for crop up to 20 days old. On older crop (up to 40 days old crop only), the amount of water used per acre needs to be increased up to 200 litres with corresponding increase in dose of insecticide. For effective control, direct the nozzle towards the whorl.  - Do not harvest fodder for 21 days after insecticide application to ensure safety to farm animals.   Application Guidelines and Safety: Apply insecticides during early morning or late evening hours to minimize bee exposure and prevent phytotoxicity from high temperatures. Ensure complete crop coverage using high-volume sprayers with appropriate nozzles. Maintain proper spray pressure and walking speed for uniform distribution.   MANDATORY SAFETY PRECAUTIONS: Always wear complete Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) including gloves, face mask, protective clothing, and boots during pesticide handling and application. Never eat, drink, or smoke while spraying. Wash thoroughly with soap and water after application.   Pre-Harvest Interval (PHI) for Fodder: This is critically important for animal safety. Observe minimum 15-20 days interval between last insecticide application and fodder harvesting, though specific PHI varies by product. Consult product labels for exact waiting periods. Feeding contaminated fodder can cause pesticide residues in milk and meat, posing serious health risks.   For precise pest identification, customized treatment recommendations based on your specific field conditions, and updated PHI information, please contact your local Agricultural Extension Officer immediately.

For effective control, the attacked plants should be uprooted and destroyed after 2-3 weeks of sowing. -  It can also be controlled with spray of 40 mL chlorantraniliprole 18.5 SC in 60-80 litres of water per acre Do not feed the fodder for atleast 21 days after the spray of chlorantraniliprole 18.5 SC .  - Alternatively use tricho-cards twice having 50,000 eggs of Corcyra cephalonica per acre parasitized by Trichogramma chilonis; first release on 10 days old crop and second one week after first release. -  Cut tricho-cards into 50 strips, each having approximately 1000 parasitized eggs. Place these strips in the central whorl uniformly at 50 spots per acre during evening hours. These tricho-cards are available at the Biocontrol Labs, Department of Entomology, PAU Ludhiana and Regional Station, Gurdaspur.  Fall armyworm:  The young larvae feed by scrapping the leaf surface making papery windows. The bigger larvae feed voraciously on the central whorl leaves causing round to oblong holes and produce a large amount of faecal matter. The larva can be identified by predominant white-coloured inverted Y-shaped mark on the head and presence of four spots arranged in square pattern at the tail end.   Adopt the following control measures: • Avoid staggered sowing in adjacent fields. • Prefer mixed cropping of fodder maize with cowpea/ bajra/ sorghum. • Use recommended seed rate (30 kg per acre) and follow line sowing method rather than broadcasting. • Regularly monitor the field to collect and destroy egg masses of fall armyworm from leaves. Egg masses are covered with hairs and are easily visible. • Spray the crop with chlorantraniliprole 18.5 SC @ 0.4 mL per litre using120 litres of water per acre, for crop up to 20 days old. On older crop (up to 40 days old crop only), the amount of water used per acre needs to be increased up to 200 litres with corresponding increase in dose of insecticide. For effective control, direct the nozzle towards the whorl.  - Do not harvest fodder for 21 days after insecticide application to ensure safety to farm animals.   Application Guidelines and Safety: Apply insecticides during early morning or late evening hours to minimize bee exposure and prevent phytotoxicity from high temperatures. Ensure complete crop coverage using high-volume sprayers with appropriate nozzles. Maintain proper spray pressure and walking speed for uniform distribution.   MANDATORY SAFETY PRECAUTIONS: Always wear complete Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) including gloves, face mask, protective clothing, and boots during pesticide handling and application. Never eat, drink, or smoke while spraying. Wash thoroughly with soap and water after application.   Pre-Harvest Interval (PHI) for Fodder: This is critically important for animal safety. Observe minimum 15-20 days interval between last insecticide application and fodder harvesting, though specific PHI varies by product. Consult product labels for exact waiting periods. Feeding contaminated fodder can cause pesticide residues in milk and meat, posing serious health risks.   For precise pest identification, customized treatment recommendations based on your specific field conditions, and updated PHI information, please contact your local Agricultural Extension Officer immediately.

generated_question: My potato plants are getting brown spots on the leaves, what can I do?
generated_answer: The brown spots on your potato leaves are likely from early blight. This disease starts with small brown spots that grow bigger and look like bull's eyes. It also affects the tubers, making them shriveled and dry. To manage it, use disease-free seeds and give balanced fertilizers. Spray 1% urea at 45 days after sowing and repeat every 8-10 days. You should also spray Mancozeb, Chlorothalonil, or Copper Oxychloride as directed. Make sure to spray every 14 days after the first application.
seed_question: How to control Early Blight in Potato?
seed_answer: Early blight in potato, caused by Alternaria solani, is one of the destructive diseases that affects potato. The disease mainly infects leaves and tubers. Initially, the symptoms occur on the lower and older leaves in the form of small (1-2 mm) circular to oval brown spots. These lesions have the tendency to become large and angular at a later stage. Mature lesions on foliage look dry and papery and often have concentric rings, looking like bulls' eyes. The symptoms on the tuber comprise brown, circular to irregular, and depressed lesions with flesh turning dry, brown, and corky. Lesions tend to enlarge during storage, and affected tubers later become shriveled. To control this disease, an integrated disease management approach is recommended. Use of disease-free seed tubers for raising the crop. The crop must be given balanced doses of fertilizers, especially nitrogen. Spraying the crop with 1% urea (10 g per liter of water, or 2 kg in 200 liters of water per acre) at 45 days after sowing and giving subsequent sprays 8-10 days after the first spray helps the crop to easily escape the severe onslaught of early blight disease. Chemical spray is very much necessary to prevent this disease. Spray Mancozeb 75% WP at 600-800 g/acre in 300 liters of water, or Chlorothalonil 75% WP at 350-500 g/acre in 240-320 liters of water per acre, or Copper Oxychloride 50% WP at 1 kg in 300-400 liters of water per acre. The second spray should be applied 14 days after the first spray.

generated_question: My paddy plants have yellowish lines on the leaves, and some of them are drying up. What can I do?
generated_answer: The yellowish lines on your paddy leaves are a sign of bacterial leaf blight. The leaves may dry up completely if it gets worse. To manage this, plant resistant varieties like Punjab Basmati 7, 5, or Pusa Basmati 1847. Use healthy seeds and avoid planting near wheat straw. Don't use too much nitrogen, and don't keep water standing in the field all the time. You can also spray copper hydroxide or copper sulphate to control the disease.
seed_question: What are the control measures for Bacterial Leaf Blight (BLB) in Paddy Basmati?
seed_answer: Bacterial blight (Xanthomonas oryzae pv. oryzae): Greenish-yellow stripes appear along the leaf margins and extend both lengthwise and breadthwise. The leaf starts drying from the tip, becomes white in severe cases and dries up completely. The disease sometimes attacks the freshly transplanted seedlings which start wilting and in a few days the whole clump dries up. The bacterium perpetuates through seed, rice straw and roots of non-host plants during the off-season. In order to mitigate the losses, adopt the following integrated measures: For the management of bacterial blight, grow basmati rice varieties Punjab Basmati 7, 5 and Pusa Basmati 1847, 1718 which are resistant to most of the pathotypes of bacterial blight pathogen. Use disease free seed, do not grow nursery and crop under shade and near wheat straw stack (Kup), do not apply excessive dose of nitrogen, nitrogen should not be applied beyond six weeks after transplanting (except when LCC is used) and do not pond water in the field continuously. Chemical control:- use of copper hydroxide 53.8% DF @600ml in 200 litres of water for one acre and PHI is 10 days or use Copper sulphate pentahydrate 6% SC @400ml in 200 litres of water for one acre and PHI 21 days and treat seeds with streptocycline 40ppm solution and soak seeds for 12 hours at room temperature before sowing.

generated_question: My tubewell isn't working, and I can't water my wheat. What can I do?
generated_answer: If your tubewell stops working and you can't water your wheat, act fast. First, check with nearby farmers to share water or hire a water tanker. seed_question: What should I do if my tubewell has stopped working and I cannot irrigate my wheat crop?
seed_answer: When your tubewell fails during the wheat-growing season in Kanpur Dehat, Uttar Pradesh, it creates a critical irrigation problem that requires immediate attention, as wheat is highly sensitive to water stress at certain growth stages.  Understanding Critical Irrigation Stages for Wheat:  Wheat requires 4-6 irrigations depending on soil type and variety. The most critical stages are: Crown Root Initiation (CRI) stage (20-25 days after sowing), Tillering stage (40-45 days), Flowering/Heading stage (60-65 days), Milk stage (80-85 days), and Dough stage (100-105 days). Technical Assessment of Tubewell:  - Check electrical connections and starter for electric tubewells  - Inspect diesel engine/motor functionality  - Examine submersible pump for mechanical failure  - Verify if the water table has dropped below pump depth  - Check delivery pipes for blockages or leakages  - Assess if the borewell has collapsed or filled with silt/sand  3. Financial Assistance Options:  - Check for subsidy schemes under Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee Yojana (PMKSY).  - Contact the District Agriculture Office for emergency irrigation support  - Approach local banks for Kisan Credit Card emergency loans  - Inquire about crop insurance if significant damage occurs   6. generated_question: My wheat plants are getting yellow leaves, what's going on?
generated_answer: The yellowing of your wheat leaves is likely due to a lack of nitrogen, which is an important nutrient for the plant. Nitrogen helps the leaves stay green and supports growth. If the older leaves start turning yellow from the tips, that's a sign of nitrogen deficiency. To fix this, you can spray a 3% urea solution. For one acre, mix 9 kg of urea in 300 liters of water and spray it crosswise over the crop. Also, make sure to test your soil before applying any fertilizer so you know exactly what your field needs. If the yellowing doesn't go away, check for other problems like waterlogging or disease.
seed_question: What causes yellowing of leaves in wheat due to urea deficiency and how can it be treated?
seed_answer: Yellowing of leaves in wheat is a common symptom of nitrogen deficiency, as urea is the primary source of nitrogen fertilizer used in wheat cultivation. Nitrogen is a crucial macronutrient essential for chlorophyll formation, protein synthesis, and overall plant growth and development.  Understanding Nitrogen Deficiency Symptoms: Nitrogen deficiency in wheat typically manifests as chlorosis (yellowing) of older leaves first, as nitrogen is a mobile nutrient that translocates from older to younger tissues when supply is limited. The yellowing usually starts from the leaf tips and progresses toward the base, eventually affecting the entire leaf. In severe cases, leaves may turn completely yellow and then brown, leading to premature senescence. This deficiency significantly impacts tillering, reduces plant vigor, and ultimately decreases grain yield and protein content.  Importance of Soil Testing: Before applying any fertilizer, farmers should conduct soil testing to determine the exact nutrient status of their fields. Soil testing helps in making informed decisions about fertilizer application rates and prevents over-application, which can lead to environmental pollution and economic losses. Contact your nearest soil testing laboratory or Krishi Vigyan Kendra for this service.  Foliar Application for Quick Correction: Spray a 3% urea solution by dissolving 3 kg of urea in 100 litres of water. For thorough crop coverage, spraying should be done in a crosswise direction. For one acre, prepare 300 litres of spray solution by dissolving 9 kg of urea in 300 litres of water. Apply the solution uniformly over the crop, spraying in a crosswise direction to ensure thorough coverage.   Comprehensive Nitrogen Management in Wheat: For optimal wheat production in Punjab conditions, follow these nitrogen management practices:  1. Basal Application: Apply 20 kg of urea per acre when SSP (Single Super Phosphate) is used instead of DAP. However, if DAP (Diammonium Phosphate) is applied, additional urea at this stage can be skipped, as DAP already supplies nitrogen.  2. Crown Root Initiation (CRI) Stage: Broadcast 45 kg urea for timely sown crop and 35 kg per acre for wheat crop sown after mid-December each with first irrigation.   3. Tillering to Jointing Stage: Broadcast 45 kg urea for timely sown crops and 35 kg per acre for wheat crops sown after mid-December, each with second irrigation. If the second irrigation is delayed due to rains, the urea should be broadcasted at 55 days after sowing. But, if water stagnates near the second irrigation, do not broadcast the second dose of urea in the soil. Instead, give two sprays (criss-cross) of 7.5% urea solution (15 kg urea in 200 liters of water per acre) at 42 and 54 days after sowing.   Application Methods: For soil application of urea, broadcast the fertilizer uniformly and incorporate it into the soil through light irrigation. Split application is more efficient than single-dose application, as it reduces nitrogen losses through leaching and volatilization.  Precautions and Safety Measures: - Wear gloves while handling urea to prevent skin irritation.  - Avoid applying urea on waterlogged soil.  - Do not apply immediately before heavy rainfall to prevent nitrogen loss.  - Store urea in moisture-proof containers away from moisture.  - Never mix urea with other chemicals without expert advice.  - Ensure proper calibration of spray equipment for uniform application  Monitoring and Follow-up: After foliar application, monitor the crop regularly for improvement in leaf color. If yellowing persists after 10-15 days, reassess the situation, as other factors like waterlogging, disease, or micronutrient deficiencies might be involved.  For further guidance or clarification on nitrogen management or soil testing procedures, or if symptoms persist despite treatment, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer for personalized recommendations based on field conditions.

If the soil doesn't have enough nitrogen, the leaves start to turn yellow, especially from the tips toward the base. To fix this, you can spray a 3% urea solution. Mix 9 kg of urea in 300 liters of water and spray it over one acre. Make sure to spray in a crosswise direction for even coverage. You can also apply urea in stages during key growth stages like CRI and tillering. Always test the soil first to know exactly what your crop needs.
seed_question: What causes yellowing of leaves in wheat due to urea deficiency and how can it be treated?
seed_answer: Yellowing of leaves in wheat is a common symptom of nitrogen deficiency, as urea is the primary source of nitrogen fertilizer used in wheat cultivation. Nitrogen is a crucial macronutrient essential for chlorophyll formation, protein synthesis, and overall plant growth and development.  Understanding Nitrogen Deficiency Symptoms: Nitrogen deficiency in wheat typically manifests as chlorosis (yellowing) of older leaves first, as nitrogen is a mobile nutrient that translocates from older to younger tissues when supply is limited. The yellowing usually starts from the leaf tips and progresses toward the base, eventually affecting the entire leaf. In severe cases, leaves may turn completely yellow and then brown, leading to premature senescence. This deficiency significantly impacts tillering, reduces plant vigor, and ultimately decreases grain yield and protein content.  Importance of Soil Testing: Before applying any fertilizer, farmers should conduct soil testing to determine the exact nutrient status of their fields. Soil testing helps in making informed decisions about fertilizer application rates and prevents over-application, which can lead to environmental pollution and economic losses. Contact your nearest soil testing laboratory or Krishi Vigyan Kendra for this service.  Foliar Application for Quick Correction: Spray a 3% urea solution by dissolving 3 kg of urea in 100 litres of water. For thorough crop coverage, spraying should be done in a crosswise direction. For one acre, prepare 300 litres of spray solution by dissolving 9 kg of urea in 300 litres of water. Apply the solution uniformly over the crop, spraying in a crosswise direction to ensure thorough coverage.   Comprehensive Nitrogen Management in Wheat: For optimal wheat production in Punjab conditions, follow these nitrogen management practices:  1. Basal Application: Apply 20 kg of urea per acre when SSP (Single Super Phosphate) is used instead of DAP. However, if DAP (Diammonium Phosphate) is applied, additional urea at this stage can be skipped, as DAP already supplies nitrogen.  2. Crown Root Initiation (CRI) Stage: Broadcast 45 kg urea for timely sown crop and 35 kg per acre for wheat crop sown after mid-December each with first irrigation.   3. Tillering to Jointing Stage: Broadcast 45 kg urea for timely sown crops and 35 kg per acre for wheat crops sown after mid-December, each with second irrigation. If the second irrigation is delayed due to rains, the urea should be broadcasted at 55 days after sowing. But, if water stagnates near the second irrigation, do not broadcast the second dose of urea in the soil. Instead, give two sprays (criss-cross) of 7.5% urea solution (15 kg urea in 200 liters of water per acre) at 42 and 54 days after sowing.   Application Methods: For soil application of urea, broadcast the fertilizer uniformly and incorporate it into the soil through light irrigation. Split application is more efficient than single-dose application, as it reduces nitrogen losses through leaching and volatilization.  Precautions and Safety Measures: - Wear gloves while handling urea to prevent skin irritation.  - Avoid applying urea on waterlogged soil.  - Do not apply immediately before heavy rainfall to prevent nitrogen loss.  - Store urea in moisture-proof containers away from moisture.  - Never mix urea with other chemicals without expert advice.  - Ensure proper calibration of spray equipment for uniform application  Monitoring and Follow-up: After foliar application, monitor the crop regularly for improvement in leaf color. If yellowing persists after 10-15 days, reassess the situation, as other factors like waterlogging, disease, or micronutrient deficiencies might be involved.  For further guidance or clarification on nitrogen management or soil testing procedures, or if symptoms persist despite treatment, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer for personalized recommendations based on field conditions.

generated_question: How much fertilizer should I use for my wheat crop, and when should I apply it?
generated_answer: For wheat, you should use 120-150 kg of nitrogen, 60 kg of phosphorus, and 40 kg of potassium per hectare. Apply all the phosphorus and potassium at sowing, along with 25% of the nitrogen. Then apply half the remaining nitrogen 20-25 days after sowing, and the last quarter at 40-45 days. Use 130 kg of DAP and 65 kg of muriate of potash at sowing. For the rest, use 100-120 kg of urea at the CRI stage and 65-80 kg at the tillering to jointing stage. Make sure the soil is moist when applying. You can also add 10-12 tonnes of farm yard manure before sowing.
seed_question: What are the fertilizer requirements and recommendations for wheat cultivation?
seed_answer: Proper fertilizer management is crucial for achieving optimal wheat yields and grain quality. Wheat is a nutrient-intensive crop that requires balanced nutrition throughout its growth stages, particularly in the Haryana region where soil conditions and climate play important roles in nutrient management.  **Nutrient Requirements for Wheat:** Wheat requires adequate amounts of macronutrients (Nitrogen, Phosphorus, and Potassium) and micronutrients (Zinc, Iron, and Sulphur) for healthy growth and maximum productivity. The nutrient uptake pattern varies across different growth stages, with peak demand occurring during tillering, stem elongation, and grain filling phases.  **Soil Testing Importance:** Before applying fertilizers, farmers should conduct soil testing to determine existing nutrient levels and pH. Soil testing laboratories at Krishi Vigyan Kendras or agricultural universities can provide accurate recommendations based on soil health. This practice prevents over-fertilization, saves costs, and protects the environment from nutrient leaching.  **Recommended NPK Doses for Haryana:** For irrigated wheat cultivation in Haryana, particularly in Mahendra Garh district, the general recommendation is 120-150 kg Nitrogen (N), 60 kg Phosphorus (Pâ‚‚Oâ‚…), and 40 kg Potassium (Kâ‚‚O) per hectare. For areas with sandy loam soils, potassium application becomes more important. These recommendations may vary based on soil test results and previous cropping history.  **Application Schedule and Method:**  **Basal Application (At Sowing):** Apply the entire dose of Phosphorus (60 kg Pâ‚‚Oâ‚…/ha) and Potassium (40 kg Kâ‚‚O/ha) along with 25% of Nitrogen (30-37.5 kg N/ha) at the time of sowing. This can be achieved by applying 130 kg DAP (Di-ammonium Phosphate) and 65 kg Muriate of Potash per hectare. Place fertilizers 5-7 cm deep in the soil below the seed using a seed-cum-fertilizer drill for better efficiency.  **First Top Dressing (CRI Stage - 21 Days After Sowing):** Apply 50% of the remaining Nitrogen (45-56 kg N/ha) at the Crown Root Initiation (CRI) stage, which occurs 20-25 days after sowing. This critical application supports root development and tillering. Use 100-120 kg Urea per hectare and apply it with irrigation for better absorption.  **Second Top Dressing (Tillering to Jointing Stage - 40-45 Days):** Apply the remaining 25% Nitrogen (30-37.5 kg N/ha) at the late tillering to early jointing stage using 65-80 kg Urea per hectare. Ensure soil moisture is adequate during application.  **Organic Fertilizer Options:** Integrating organic sources enhances soil health and provides sustained nutrient release. Apply 10-12 tonnes of well-decomposed Farm Yard Manure (FYM) or compost per hectare 2-3 weeks before sowing. Incorporation of green manure crops like dhaincha or sunhemp in the previous season adds nitrogen and improves soil structure. Vermicompost at 2.5-3 tonnes per hectare can also supplement chemical fertilizers.  **Micronutrient Management:** Zinc deficiency is common in Haryana soils, particularly in alkaline conditions. Apply Zinc Sulphate (ZnSOâ‚„) @ 25 kg/ha once every 2-3 years or use foliar spray of 0.5% Zinc Sulphate solution at tillering and flowering stages. Iron deficiency can be corrected with Ferrous Sulphate @ 0.5% foliar spray. Sulphur application @ 20-25 kg/ha through elemental sulphur or gypsum improves protein content in grains.  **Safety Precautions for Chemical Fertilizers:** While handling chemical fertilizers, farmers should wear protective gloves and avoid direct skin contact. Store fertilizers in dry, covered spaces away from seeds and food items. Do not mix different fertilizers unless recommended, as some combinations may cause nutrient loss. Wash hands thoroughly after fertilizer application.  **Important Advisory:** Fertilizer recommendations should be adjusted based on irrigation availability, soil type, and variety selection. Late-sown wheat may require modified nitrogen management to prevent lodging. For further guidance or clarification on soil-specific fertilizer recommendations and customized nutrient management plans, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

Regional Suitability: Pro Agro 6444 is a hybrid paddy variety suitable for cultivation in the Indo-Gangetic plains, including Uttar Pradesh, particularly in districts like Faizabad (now Ayodhya). Pro Agro 6444 is a medium-duration hybrid variety with a crop duration of approximately 130-135 days from transplanting to maturity. It typically yields 23.8 quintals per acre under optimal management conditions. The variety produces medium-to-long slender grains with good cooking quality.   Disease and Pest Resistance: Pro Agro 6444 shows resistance to common paddy diseases like bacterial leaf blight, sheath blight, green leaf hopper and gall midge. generated_question: My chilli plants are getting damaged, and I see little worms eating the pods. What's going on?
generated_answer: It sounds like you're dealing with Fruit Borer, not Spotted Bollworm. The caterpillars are brown at first and turn green with dark lines as they grow. They eat the inside of the pods and leave holes. To manage them, use neem cake at transplanting, set up pheromone traps, and release Trichogramma wasps. seed_question: What are the effective methods for controlling Spotted bollworm in chili crops?
seed_answer: The Spotted Bollworm (Earias vittella or Earias insulana) is a highly destructive pest, but it specifically targets crops in the mallow family, most notably cotton and okra (bhindi). It does not attack chilli crops.  If you are seeing caterpillars boring into your chilli pods, hollowing them out, or eating the tender upper shoots, you are dealing with the Fruit Borer.  Identification   Caterpillars vary in colour, initially brown and later turn greenish with darker broken lines along the side of the body. The larval period lasts for 18-25 days. Body covered with radiating hairs. When full-grown, they measure 3.7 to 5 cm in length. The full-grown caterpillar pupates in the soil in an earthen cell and emerges in 16-21 days  Symptoms: Young larva feeds on the leaves for some time and then attacks fruits. Internal tissues are eaten severely and completely hollowed out. While feeding, the caterpillar thrust its head inside, leaving the rest of the body outside. Bored fruits with round holes.Fed leaves, shoots and buds.  Cultural control:  - Field sanitation and roguin.  - Erecting suitable physical barriers, such as nylon nets  - Growing intercrops such as cowpea, onion, maize, coriander, urdbean in 5 or 4:1 ratio  - Guard crop sorghum or maize in 4 rows all around the cotton crop as a guard crop.  - Rotate the chilli crop with a non-host cereal crop, cucurbit, or cruciferous vegetable.  - Repellant plants: Ocimum/Basil  - Erecting of bird perches for encouraging predatory birds such as the king crow, mynah, and drongo, etc.  - Install pheromone traps @ 4-5/acre for monitoring adult moth activity. Replace the lures with fresh lures every 2-3 weeks  - Use of ovipositional trap crops such as marigold @ 100 plants/acre, 1 row of marigold for every 18 rows of chilli, and collection of larvae from flowers (marigold seedlings of 45 days should be planted along with chilli transplanting)  Biological control:  - Release of egg parasitoid Trichogramma pretiosum @ 50,000 adults (in the form of parasitised card)/acre/week commenced right from the start of flower initiation to till the end of the crop, tie the egg cards on the stick placed throughout the field at 4-5 m apart, in the evening, a day before the emergence of adults.  - Conserve parasitoids such as Tetrastichus spp. (egg), Telenomus spp. (egg), Campoletis chlorideae (larval), etc.  - Conserve predators such as Chrysoperla carnea, coccinellids, King crow, common mynah, wasp, dragonfly, spider, robber fly, reduviid bug, praying mantis, fire ants, big-eyed bugs (Geocoris sp), pentatomid bug (Eocanthecona furcellata), earwigs, ground beetles, rove beetles, etc. -Apply neem cake @ 100 kg/acre at the time of transplanting for reducing borer damage.  Light traps  Set up light traps 1 trap/acre 15 cm above the crop canopy for monitoring and mass trapping insects. Light traps with an exit option for natural enemies of smaller size should be installed and operated around dusk time (6 pm to 10 pm).  Chemical Control Measures:  For effective chemical management, initiate spray applications at the first sign of pest activity or during the vulnerable crop stage. Spray 50 ml Chlorantraniliprole 18.5 SC or 50 ml Spinosad 45 SC  in 100 litres of water per acre, ensuring thorough coverage of foliage and developing fruits. Rotate insecticides with different modes of action to prevent resistance development. Maintain a spray interval of 12-15 days based on pest pressure.  Safety Precautions:  When handling and applying chemical insecticides, always wear protective equipment, including gloves, full-sleeved shirts, long pants, boots, a face mask, and safety goggles. Avoid spraying during windy conditions or peak sunlight hours. Strictly observe the Pre-Harvest Interval (PHI) of 3-5 days, depending on the specific insecticide used, before harvesting fruits for consumption. Never eat, drink, or smoke during application. Store pesticides in original containers away from food items and out of children's reach.  For further guidance or clarification, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

generated_question: How much sulphur should I use for my cotton crop?
generated_answer: For hybrid or Bt cotton, you should use 150 kg of SSP per acre. For American cotton, 75 kg of SSP per acre is enough. SSP has 11–12% sulphur, so this gives around 16–18 kg of sulphur for hybrid cotton and 8–9 kg for American cotton. Sulphur helps with growth and prevents diseases like grey mildew and mites. For mites, you can spray 5 grams of water-soluble sulphur in a liter of water. Make sure to follow the local guidelines for best results.
seed_question: What is the recommended sulphur dose for cotton cultivation?
seed_answer: As per Haryana Package of Practices (POP) for cotton, Hybrid/Bt cotton is recommended to receive 150 kg SSP per acre, while American cotton is recommended 75 kg SSP per acre. Single Super Phosphate (SSP) contains 16% P₂O₅ and about 11–12% sulphur. Therefore, application of 150 kg SSP per acre in Hybrid/Bt cotton supplies approximately 16–18 kg sulphur per acre, whereas application of 75 kg SSP per acre in American cotton supplies about 8–9 kg sulphur per acre through SSP.  Sulphur is an essential secondary nutrient required for optimal cotton (Kapas) growth and development. As per the POP, Sulphur is given in cotton as a control measure against certain diseases which are as following:    - Grey mildew: For desi cotton, the recommended Sulphur application is 10 kg/acre for the control of Grey mildew disease.   - Mites: Spray water soluble sulphur @5 g per litre of water is effective against diseases spread by mites.    For further guidance or clarification on sulphur management, soil testing facilities, or specific fertilizer recommendations for your field conditions, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

Therefore, application of 150 kg SSP per acre in Hybrid/Bt cotton supplies approximately 16–18 kg sulphur per acre, whereas application of 75 kg SSP per acre in American cotton supplies about 8–9 kg sulphur per acre through SSP.  Sulphur is an essential secondary nutrient required for optimal cotton (Kapas) growth and development. As per the POP, Sulphur is given in cotton as a control measure against certain diseases which are as following:    - Grey mildew: For desi cotton, the recommended Sulphur application is 10 kg/acre for the control of Grey mildew disease.   - Mites: Spray water soluble sulphur @5 g per litre of water is effective against diseases spread by mites.    For further guidance or clarification on sulphur management, soil testing facilities, or specific fertilizer recommendations for your field conditions, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

seed_question: What are the effective methods for controlling diseases in wheat crop?
seed_answer: Wheat crops are susceptible to various fungal and bacterial diseases that can significantly reduce yield and grain quality if not managed properly. The most common diseases affecting wheat in Haryana include leaf rust (brown rust), yellow rust (stripe rust), stem rust (black rust), powdery mildew, karnal bunt, and loose smut. Effective disease management requires a combination of preventive measures and timely intervention with appropriate fungicides.   Common Wheat Diseases and Symptoms: Leaf rust appears as orange-brown pustules on leaf surfaces, while yellow rust shows yellow-orange stripes along leaf veins. Powdery mildew manifests as white, powdery growth on leaves and stems. Karnal bunt affects grain quality with black powdery spores inside kernels. Early detection of these symptoms is crucial for effective management.   Chemical Control Measures: For broad-spectrum fungal disease control in wheat, Mancozeb 75% WP is highly effective. Apply at 800 grams per acre mixed in 200-250 liters of water. For rust diseases specifically, use Propiconazole 25% EC at 200 ml per acre with 200 lit of water or Tebuconazole 25% WG at 300 g per acre in 200 litres of water. For powdery mildew, apply Sulfur 80% WP at 800-1000 g per acre.   The timing of the application is critical. First spray should be applied at disease appearance or as a preventive measure at tillering to boot leaf stage. A second spray may be required 15-20 days after the first spray if disease pressure persists. For Karnal bunt specifically, spray at 50% flowering (anthesis stage) with Propiconazole or Tebuconazole.   Biological Control: Use Trichoderma viride or Trichoderma harzianum for seed treatment at 6-10 grams per kg of seed. This protects against seed and soil-borne diseases. Pseudomonas fluorescens can be applied as a foliar spray at 10 grams per liter of water for managing leaf diseases.   Cultural Practices: Plant disease-resistant varieties like HD-2967, WH-1105, DBW-187, or PBW-725, which show good resistance to rust diseases. Follow crop rotation with non-cereal crops to break disease cycles. Ensure proper seed treatment with fungicides like Carboxin 37.5% + Thiram 37.5% WS at 2.5-3.0 grams per kg of seed before sowing. Remove crop residues and volunteer plants that can harbor pathogens. Maintain optimal plant spacing and avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization,n which can increase disease susceptibility. Ensure proper drainage to prevent waterlogging that favors disease development.   Safety Precautions: When applying chemical fungicides, farmers must wear protective equipment, including gloves, a face mask or respirator, goggles, long-sleeved shirts, and full-length pants. Apply fungicides during early morning or late evening hours when the temperature is moderate and the wind speed is low. Observe the pre-harvest interval (PHI) for most fungicides before harvesting grain. Never spray near water bodies, and dispose of empty containers safely by puncturing and burying them away from water sources. Wash your hands and exposed skin thoroughly with soap and water after handling chemicals.   Integrated Disease Management: Combine chemical, biological, and cultural methods for sustainable disease control. Regular field monitoring for early disease detection, use of certified disease-free seeds, balanced fertilization, and timely irrigation management form the foundation of integrated disease management in wheat.   For further guidance on specific disease identification, appropriate fungicide selection, or clarification on control measures, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer for region-specific recommendations.

crop: Indian rapeseed and mustard yellow sarson
generated_question: My mustard plants are getting damaged by tiny bugs, what can I do?
generated_answer: The tiny bugs are aphids, which suck the sap from your mustard plants. They can make the plants weak, cause leaves to curl, and stop the seeds from developing. To control them, try sowing early, use resistant varieties, and set up yellow sticky traps. If the aphid count gets high, spray insecticides like thiamethoxam or dimethoate. Make sure to check your plants regularly and spray when needed. Always follow the recommended doses and rotate insecticides to avoid resistance.
seed_question: What are the control measures for aphid infestation in mustard (Raya)?
seed_answer: Aphid is a significant sucking pest affecting mustard (Raya/Sarson) cultivation in Punjab, causing considerable yield losses if not managed timely. Cold and cloudy weather is very favourable for the development of mustard aphid. The green plant lice become innumerable, covering the inflorescence and siliquae   Description and Symptoms: Both nymph and adults suck the sap from leaves, buds and pods. They suck the plant sap in huge quantities and as a result, the plants remain stunted, siliquae shrivel up and seeds do not develop. Curling may occur for infested leaves and at advanced stage plants may wither and die. Plants remain stunted and sooty molds grow on the honey dew excreted by the insects.  Integrated Management Practices: Cultural Control: - Early sowing to avoid damage due to mustard-aphid, and major diseases preferably upto 3rd week of October. - Use tolerant varieties. - Early planting to escape the damage. - Use yellow sticky traps. Set up yellow water pan/sticky traps 15 cm above the canopy for monitoring aphids @ 4-5 traps/acre. Locally available empty tins can be painted yellow and coated with grease/ Vaseline/castor oil on outer surface may also be used as yellow sticky trap.  Mechanical control: - Destroy the affected part along with aphid population in the initial stage.  Chemical control: Apply insecticides on the basis of need by following any of the economic threshold levels given below. For determining aphid infestation, observe 12-16 widely scattered plants from an acre twice a week starting from first week of January. - Spray, when pest population reaches, 50-60 aphids per 10 cm terminal portion of the central shoot or - When an average of 0.5 to 1.0 cm terminal portion of central shoot is covered by aphids or - When plants infestation by aphids reach 40-50% (observe 100 plants per acre).  Spray any one of the following insecticides using 80–125 litres of water per acre, depending upon the stage of the crop, and repeat the application whenever the above-mentioned economic threshold levels are observed. - Thiamethoxam 25% WG @ 40 g/acre - Oxydemeton–methyl 25% EC @ 400 ml/acre  - Dimethoate 30% EC @ 400 ml/acre - Chlorpyrifos 20 % EC @ 600 ml/acre  Rotate insecticides with different modes of action to prevent development of resistance. Apply sprays during early morning or late evening hours for maximum effectiveness.   For further guidance or clarification on whitefly management, proper identification, spray schedules, or integrated pest management strategies specific to your location, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

Curling may occur for infested leaves and at advanced stage plants may wither and die. Plants remain stunted and sooty molds grow on the honey dew excreted by the insects.  Integrated Management Practices: Cultural Control: - Early sowing to avoid damage due to mustard-aphid, and major diseases preferably upto 3rd week of October. - Use tolerant varieties. - Early planting to escape the damage. - Use yellow sticky traps. Set up yellow water pan/sticky traps 15 cm above the canopy for monitoring aphids @ 4-5 traps/acre. Locally available empty tins can be painted yellow and coated with grease/ Vaseline/castor oil on outer surface may also be used as yellow sticky trap.  Mechanical control: - Destroy the affected part along with aphid population in the initial stage.  Chemical control: Apply insecticides on the basis of need by following any of the economic threshold levels given below. For determining aphid infestation, observe 12-16 widely scattered plants from an acre twice a week starting from first week of January. - Spray, when pest population reaches, 50-60 aphids per 10 cm terminal portion of the central shoot or - When an average of 0.5 to 1.0 cm terminal portion of central shoot is covered by aphids or - When plants infestation by aphids reach 40-50% (observe 100 plants per acre).  Spray any one of the following insecticides using 80–125 litres of water per acre, depending upon the stage of the crop, and repeat the application whenever the above-mentioned economic threshold levels are observed. - Thiamethoxam 25% WG @ 40 g/acre - Oxydemeton–methyl 25% EC @ 400 ml/acre  - Dimethoate 30% EC @ 400 ml/acre - Chlorpyrifos 20 % EC @ 600 ml/acre  Rotate insecticides with different modes of action to prevent development of resistance. Apply sprays during early morning or late evening hours for maximum effectiveness.   For further guidance or clarification on whitefly management, proper identification, spray schedules, or integrated pest management strategies specific to your location, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

generated_question: My paddy plants are really short, what can I do to fix it?
generated_answer: You can try a few things. First, use the PAU leaf color chart to check if your plants need urea. Apply 25 kg of urea per acre if the leaves are lighter than shade 4 on the chart. Keep checking every 7 days, starting 14 days after transplanting. Make sure you have 2-3 inches of water in the field. Test the soil pH and add lime if it's too acidic. Also, check for zinc deficiency and apply zinc sulfate if needed. Use good quality seeds and plant them 20 x 15 cm apart. Add farmyard manure before transplanting. Watch for pests like stem borer and spray if needed. Don't use old seedlings or too much herbicide.
seed_question: How can I improve the growth of paddy crop when facing dwarf plant issues in Punjab?
seed_answer: Dwarf plant issues in paddy can be addressed through several methods:  1) Use the PAU leaf color chart for need-based urea application. Apply a basal dose of 25 kg of urea per acre. Begin matching the color of the first fully exposed leaf from the top with the Leaf Color Chart (LCC) at 7-day intervals, starting 14 days after transplanting. Whenever the greenness of six or more out of ten leaves is lighter than LCC shade 4, apply 25 kg urea per acre. Do not apply urea if the leaf color is equal to or darker than LCC shade 4. Discontinue the use of the LCC after the initiation of flowering, and do not apply any further urea thereafter. 2) Ensure proper water management with 2-3 inches of standing water during active growth stages.  3) Test soil pH- paddy grows best in pH 5.5-6.5; apply lime if the soil is too acidic.  4) Check for zinc deficiency (common in Punjab soils) and apply 25 kg of zinc sulfate heptahydrate (21%) or 16 kg of zinc sulfate monohydrate (33%) per acre at puddling in case the previous crop in this field had shown the symptoms of zinc deficiency. 5) Use recommended varieties for Punjab, like PR 126, PR 129, PR 128, PR 131, or PR 130. 6) Maintain proper plant spacing of 20 x 15 cm for better growth.  7) Apply farmyard manure at 6 tonnes/acre before transplanting to improve soil health and growth. 8) Check for insect pests, especially stem borer. Early stem borer attack causes dead hearts, reduces tillering, and results in short plants. If dead hearts exceed the ETL (5% in non-basmati, 2% in basmati), spray Chlorantraniliprole 18.5 SC @ 60 ml with 200 litre of water per acre or other PAU-recommended insecticides. 9) Avoid stress factors such as late transplanting, very old seedlings, herbicide injury, or cold water from tubewells during early growth. Always use 25–30 day old seedlings, follow recommended herbicide doses, and avoid mixing chemicals unnecessarily.

seed_question: Provide information on the yield of Pusa Basmati 1692 (PB 1692) per acre ?
seed_answer: Pusa Basmati 1692 (PB 1692) is an early-maturing Basmati rice variety that is well suited for Basmati-growing regions. It matures quickly in about 110–115 days from seed to seed and has a semi-dwarf plant type with good resistance to lodging and grain shattering. Because of its early maturity, farmers can harvest the crop on time, clear the fields early, and prepare land properly for the next wheat crop, which also helps reduce environmental pollution caused by delayed harvesting. Under normal management practices, Pusa Basmati 1692 gives an average yield of about 20 to 24 quintals per acre. With good crop management, proper fertilizer use, and timely irrigation, farmers can achieve stable and profitable yields. For transplanting, about 5 kg of seed per acre is sufficient to raise a healthy nursery, making this variety economical and farmer-friendly.

generated_question: My berseem crop is getting some kind of disease, and the stems are rotting. What can I do about it?
generated_answer: The disease is called stem rot, and it's caused by a fungus. It usually shows up in January and February. The fungus lives in the soil as hard black seeds and attacks the stem, making it rot. You can see white, cottony growth around the affected plants. To control it, flood the field heavily in summer to kill the fungus. After cutting the crop, let the soil dry in the sun. Also, don't plant berseem in the same field for 3–4 years. Keep checking your fields regularly so you can act quickly if you see any signs.
seed_question: What are the integrated management practices for controlling stem rot disease in berseem?
seed_answer: Stem rot is a significant fungal disease affecting berseem (Trifolium alexandrinum), commonly caused by Sclerotinia sclerotiorum and occasionally by Rhizoctonia solani. This disease can cause substantial yield losses if not managed properly through integrated approaches combining cultural, biological and chemical control methods.   Stem Rot (Sclerotinia sclerotiorum): The symptoms of this disease usually appear during January and February when the temperature is low. The fungus survives in the soil as sclerotia and attacks the basal portion of the stem, causing it to rot. It produces white, cottony mycelium that grows on dead organic matter on the soil surface. This white mycelium can be easily seen in the field around the wilted patches of the berseem crop.  To manage the disease, the affected field should be heavily flooded during the summer months to destroy the hard, black sclerotia present in the soil. After cutting the crop, expose the soil to sunlight. Also, avoid growing berseem in heavily infested fields for 3–4 years.   Monitoring and Timing : Regularly scout fields, especially during favorable disease conditions. Early detection and immediate intervention significantly improve management effectiveness and reduce yield losses.   For further guidance or clarification, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

seed_answer: Stem rot is a significant fungal disease affecting berseem (Trifolium alexandrinum), commonly caused by Sclerotinia sclerotiorum and occasionally by Rhizoctonia solani. This disease can cause substantial yield losses if not managed properly through integrated approaches combining cultural, biological and chemical control methods.   Stem Rot (Sclerotinia sclerotiorum): The symptoms of this disease usually appear during January and February when the temperature is low. The fungus survives in the soil as sclerotia and attacks the basal portion of the stem, causing it to rot. It produces white, cottony mycelium that grows on dead organic matter on the soil surface. This white mycelium can be easily seen in the field around the wilted patches of the berseem crop.  To manage the disease, the affected field should be heavily flooded during the summer months to destroy the hard, black sclerotia present in the soil. After cutting the crop, expose the soil to sunlight. Also, avoid growing berseem in heavily infested fields for 3–4 years.   Monitoring and Timing : Regularly scout fields, especially during favorable disease conditions. Early detection and immediate intervention significantly improve management effectiveness and reduce yield losses.   For further guidance or clarification, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

Wear gloves and a mask while applying. You can also hoe the field 3-4 times, starting after 3 weeks. Make sure the seedbed is clean and use good quality seeds. seed_question: What are the effective methods for pre-emergence weed control in onion cultivation?
seed_answer: Pre-emergence weed control is a critical component of successful onion cultivation, as onions are highly susceptible to weed competition during their early growth stages due to their slow germination, narrow upright leaves, and shallow root system. Effective weed management during the pre-emergence phase can significantly improve crop establishment, reduce labor costs, and enhance overall yield.    Understanding Pre-emergence Weed Control:  Pre-emergence weed control involves the application of herbicides after sowing but before the emergence of onion seedlings or weeds. This approach creates a protective barrier in the soil that prevents weed seed germination and early seedling development. For onion cultivation, timing is crucial as the crop takes 7-10 days to emerge after transplanting or direct seeding.    Chemical Control Methods:  Several pre-emergence herbicides have proven effective for onion crops. Pendimethalin 38.7% CS  at 600-700ml  per acre in 200 liters of water can be applied within 2-3 days after transplanting or sowing. This herbicide effectively controls annual grasses and some broadleaf weeds. The application should be made when soil moisture is adequate for optimal herbicide activation.    Oxyfluorfen 23.5% EC at 170-340ml/acre in 200-300 liters of water another effective option, particularly against broadleaf weeds and some grasses. It should be applied as a directed spray immediately after transplanting, taking care to avoid contact with onion plants.    Safety Precautions for Chemical Application:  When applying pre-emergence herbicides, farmers must wear complete protective equipment including chemical-resistant gloves, long-sleeved shirts, long pants, closed-toe shoes, and a face shield or goggles. A respirator with appropriate filters should be used during mixing and application. Avoid herbicide application on windy days to prevent drift to non-target areas. The Pre-Harvest Interval (PHI)should be observed carefully. Store herbicides in original containers in a locked, well-ventilated area away from children, animals, and food items. In case of accidental exposure, wash the affected area immediately with plenty of water and seek medical attention.    Cultural Control Practices:  Integrating cultural practices enhances pre-emergence weed control effectiveness. Weeds are controlled by giving 3-4 hoeing. Give first hoeing after 3 weeks of transplanting. Prepare a well-pulverized, weed-free seedbed before planting. Use certified, weed-free onion seeds or healthy transplants. Apply well-decomposed farmyard manure (20 tons per acre) during land preparation, as fresh manure often contains viable weed seeds. Maintain optimal soil moisture through irrigation immediately after herbicide application to activate the chemical barrier. Proper bed preparation with raised beds (15-20 cm height) improves drainage and reduces weed pressure.    Integrated Approach:  For best results, combine pre-emergence herbicides with timely hand weeding or mechanical weeding at 20-25 and 40-45 days after transplanting. Mulching with crop residues or black plastic mulch can further suppress weed emergence while conserving soil moisture. Monitor fields regularly for herbicide-resistant weeds and adjust management strategies accordingly.    For further guidance or clarification, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

generated_question: I'm growing potatoes, and my leaves are looking yellow and sick. What can I do?
generated_answer: Your potato plants might be lacking nitrogen. The yellowing is even across the whole leaf. You can spray 2% urea solution, which is 20 grams in 1 liter of water. Do this three times every two weeks. Also, make sure to use Biozyme granules and liquid at the right stages. If the leaves look scorched, it might be a magnesium problem. You can spray 0.2% MgSO4, which is 2 grams in 1 liter of water. Keep an eye on the leaves and adjust as needed.
seed_question: What are the recommended foliar spray practices for potato crops?
seed_answer: Foliar spray application is an essential agronomic practice in potato cultivation that helps maintain plant health, enhance nutrient availability, and improve overall crop productivity. Foliar nutrition serves as a supplementary feeding method that delivers nutrients directly through the leaves when soil application becomes less effective or when plants require immediate nutrient correction.  Biozyme granule and liquid formulation should be applied along with fertilizers in order to increase the yield of potato. Apply 8 Kg granule per acre each at the time of planting and earthing up and spray of Biozyme liquid formulation at tuber initiation stage @200ml/acre increases the yield of potato.  Magnesium: The Mg-deficient leaves show advanced inter veinal chlorosis. Interveinal necrosis causes scorched look. In its advanced form, magnesium deficiency may superficially resemble potassium deficiency. The symptoms generally start with mottled chlorotic areas developing in the interveinal tissue. Symptoms appear first on young mature leaves. Correction measure: Foliar application of 0.2% MgSO4 (2g per litre of water)  Nitrogen: The yellowing in nitrogen deficiency is uniform over the entire leaf including the veins. Recovery of deficient plants to applied nitrogen is immediate (days) and spectacular. Younger leaves turn darker green, older leaves remain yellow. Upward cupping of deficient when severs. Correction measure: Foliar application of 2% (20 g in 1 litre of water) urea thrice at fortnightly interval or soil application of Nitrogen based on soil test recommendation.  Potassium:  Since potassium is very mobile within the plant, symptoms only develop on young and full sized leaves in the case of extreme deficiency. Leaflets become rugose (crinkled). Some of the leaves show marginal necrosis (tip burn), and at a more advanced deficiency status show interveinal necrosis. As the deficiency progresses, most of the interveinal area becomes necrotic, the veins remain green and the leaves tend to curland crinkle. Leaves take on a scorched appearance with black pigmentation and necrtic (dead tissue) edges. In contrast to nitrogen deficiency, chlorosis is irreversible in potassium deficiency, even if potassium is given to the plants. Correction measure: Foliar application of K2SO4 @1% (10 g in 1 litre of water)  For further guidance or clarification, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

For magnesium deficiency, spray 0.2% MgSO4 (2g in 1 litre of water). For nitrogen, use 2% urea (20g in 1 litre of water) three times every two weeks. For potassium, spray 1% K2SO4 (10g in 1 litre of water).
seed_question: What are the recommended foliar spray practices for potato crops?
seed_answer: Foliar spray application is an essential agronomic practice in potato cultivation that helps maintain plant health, enhance nutrient availability, and improve overall crop productivity. Foliar nutrition serves as a supplementary feeding method that delivers nutrients directly through the leaves when soil application becomes less effective or when plants require immediate nutrient correction.  Biozyme granule and liquid formulation should be applied along with fertilizers in order to increase the yield of potato. Apply 8 Kg granule per acre each at the time of planting and earthing up and spray of Biozyme liquid formulation at tuber initiation stage @200ml/acre increases the yield of potato.  Magnesium: The Mg-deficient leaves show advanced inter veinal chlorosis. Interveinal necrosis causes scorched look. In its advanced form, magnesium deficiency may superficially resemble potassium deficiency. The symptoms generally start with mottled chlorotic areas developing in the interveinal tissue. Symptoms appear first on young mature leaves. Correction measure: Foliar application of 0.2% MgSO4 (2g per litre of water)  Nitrogen: The yellowing in nitrogen deficiency is uniform over the entire leaf including the veins. Recovery of deficient plants to applied nitrogen is immediate (days) and spectacular. Younger leaves turn darker green, older leaves remain yellow. Upward cupping of deficient when severs. Correction measure: Foliar application of 2% (20 g in 1 litre of water) urea thrice at fortnightly interval or soil application of Nitrogen based on soil test recommendation.  Potassium:  Since potassium is very mobile within the plant, symptoms only develop on young and full sized leaves in the case of extreme deficiency. Leaflets become rugose (crinkled). Some of the leaves show marginal necrosis (tip burn), and at a more advanced deficiency status show interveinal necrosis. As the deficiency progresses, most of the interveinal area becomes necrotic, the veins remain green and the leaves tend to curland crinkle. Leaves take on a scorched appearance with black pigmentation and necrtic (dead tissue) edges. In contrast to nitrogen deficiency, chlorosis is irreversible in potassium deficiency, even if potassium is given to the plants. Correction measure: Foliar application of K2SO4 @1% (10 g in 1 litre of water)  For further guidance or clarification, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

seed_question: What is the recommended dose of Paraquat Dichloride herbicide for weed control in paddy cultivation?
seed_answer: Paraquat Dichloride 24% SL is a non-selective contact herbicide used for effective weed management in paddy cultivation. Understanding its proper application is crucial for successful weed control while ensuring crop safety and environmental protection.  Herbicide Characteristics:  Paraquat Dichloride (marketed under various generic names) is a fast-acting, non-selective contact herbicide that controls a broad spectrum of annual grasses and broadleaf weeds. It works by disrupting photosynthesis in green plant tissues, causing rapid desiccation and death of exposed vegetation. Being a contact herbicide, it only affects the plant parts it touches and does not have systemic or residual soil activity. It controls weeds like Echinochloa crusgalli, Cyperus iria, Ageratum conyzoides, Commelina benghalensis, Marsilea quadriofoliata, and Brachiaria mutica in paddy.  Recommended Dosage and Application:  For paddy cultivation, the recommended dose of Paraquat Dichloride 24% SL is 0.5-1.4 liters per acre mixed in 200 liters of water. This dosage is effective for controlling emerged weeds in paddy fields. The herbicide should be applied as a directed spray, carefully avoiding contact with the paddy crop plants to prevent damage.  Timing of Application:  Paraquat dichloride is typically used as a pre-planting herbicide in paddy fields. It can be applied 5-7 days before transplanting or sowing to clear the field of existing weeds. In direct-seeded rice systems, it should be applied before rice emergence. For established crops, it can be used as a directed spray between rows, ensuring the spray does not contact the rice plants. Some farmers also use it for weed control on bunds and field margins.  Application Method:  Use a knapsack sprayer with a flat-fan nozzle for uniform coverage. Mix the required quantity of herbicide thoroughly in clean water. Apply when weeds are young and actively growing (2-4 leaf stage) for maximum effectiveness. Ensure complete coverage of weed foliage. Application should be done during calm weather conditions (morning or evening) to prevent drift onto the crop. Avoid spraying during windy conditions or when rain is expected within 6 hours.  SAFETY PRECAUTIONS (MANDATORY):  Paraquat dichloride is highly toxic and requires extreme caution during handling and application. Farmers must wear full protective equipment, including chemical-resistant gloves, a face shield or goggles, a respirator mask, long-sleeved clothing, long pants, and boots. Never eat, drink, or smoke during application. Keep children, livestock, and unauthorized persons away from the application area. After application, wash hands and exposed skin thoroughly with soap and water. Wash contaminated clothing separately before reuse. Store the herbicide in its original container in a locked, well-ventilated area away from food, feed, and children. Empty containers should be triple-rinsed and punctured before disposal according to local regulations. Never reuse empty containers for any purpose. In case of accidental exposure or poisoning, seek immediate medical attention and show the product label to the doctor.  For further guidance or clarification on herbicide selection, application techniques, or integrated weed management strategies specific to your paddy variety and local conditions, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

generated_question: My mustard plants are getting some kind of leaf disease, what can I do?
generated_answer: The disease is called downy mildew, and it's caused by a fungus. It starts with small light green spots on the bottom of the leaves, which turn grey and dry out. It affects the whole plant, especially in cool and wet weather. To manage it, use resistant seeds, avoid overwatering, and destroy old crop debris. You can also treat seeds with garlic extract. If the disease appears, spray with fungicides like Metalaxyl or Metalaxyl plus mancozeb. Make sure to follow safety measures when applying chemicals.
seed_question: What are the control measures for downy mildew disease in Gobhi Sarson (Indian mustard)?
seed_answer: Downy mildew is a serious fungal disease affecting Gobhi Sarson (Brassica napus) caused by the pathogen Hyaloperonospora parasitica (formerly Peronospora parasitica). This disease can significantly reduce crop yield and quality if not managed properly, particularly under cool and humid weather conditions prevalent in Punjab during the mustard growing season.   Disease Description and Symptoms: - Small light green lesions appear on the lower side of the leaves, which later enlarge, become more greyish, dry up, shrivel and tear easily.  - All aerial parts of the plants are attacked. The disease is severe on foliage and seed bearing parts.  - Greater deformity occurs in the stem.  - Relative humidity of more than 90% with temperature in the range of 10-20°C are conducive for disease development and spread.  - To avoid incidence of this disease, destroy diseased debris from the previous crop.  Integrated Disease Management: Cultural Practices:  • Use certified seeds of resistant/tolerant variety. • Follow timely sowing of crop. • Adopt proper field sanitation. • Follow proper crop rotation. • Destruct crop debris particularly stag heads of previous year crop. • Avoid over irrigation or water stagnation. • Apply potash in recommended dose   Botanical Management:  Treat the seeds with freshly prepared garlic bulb extract @ 2% (w/v).  Chemical Management: Fungicide application is essential when disease symptoms first appear or under favorable environmental conditions. Apply  • Metalaxyl 35% WS @ 0.75- 1.0 Kg/100 Kg seed • Metalaxyl M 4% + mancozeb 64% WP @ 1000 g in 400 l of water/acre • Metalaxyl 8% + mancozeb 64% WP @1000 g in 400 l of water/acre (white rust)   Ensure thorough coverage of both leaf surfaces during spraying. Repeat applications at 10-15 day intervals if disease persists, but do not exceed 2-3 sprays per season. Alternate between different fungicide groups to prevent resistance development.    Safety Precautions: When handling and applying fungicides, farmers must wear protective clothing including long-sleeved shirts, long pants, chemical-resistant gloves, protective goggles, and face masks to avoid skin contact and inhalation of spray mist. Mix chemicals in open, well-ventilated areas away from food and water sources. Do not eat, drink, or smoke during application. After spraying, wash hands, face, and exposed body parts thoroughly with soap and water. Wash contaminated clothing separately before reuse. Observe the Pre-Harvest Interval (PHI).   For further guidance or clarification, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

generated_question: Kya mujhe kisi chhoti bimari ke karan hatheli ke pata buri nazar aayi hai, kya karna chahiye?
generated_answer: Hatheli ke pata chhoti bimari ke karan buri nazar aayi hai, toh aap kisan kuch aasani se karo. Pehle, apne seed ko 2% garlic extract se chhaap do. Agar bimari badh rahi hai, toh fungicide lagao. Metalaxyl 35% WS ko 0.75-1.0 kg seed per 100 kg me mix karo. Aap khet me pata ke dono taraf se thoda thora spray karo. Agar bimari badh rahi hai, toh 10-15 din baad dobara karo, lekin 2-3 baar tak hi karo. Aap fungicide ko alag alag tareeke se istemal karo taake bimari resistance na ban jaye.

generated_question: Kya mujhe downy mildew ke ilaj ke liye kuch alag chiz karna padega jaise kisi specific fungicide ka istemal?
generated_answer: Haan, jab bhi disease ke lakshan dikhne lage ya jab aahar ke halat achha ho, to fungicide ka istemal jaruri hai. Metalaxyl 35% WS ko 0.75-1.0 kg 100 kg seed ke saath chhaapna chahiye. Dusre variant me, Metalaxyl M 4% + mancozeb 64% WP ko 1000 g 400 l pani me 1 acre ke liye istemal karna chahiye. Dusre chakkar me, Metalaxyl 8% + mancozeb 64% WP ko bhi 1000 g 400 l pani me 1 acre ke liye use karna chahiye. Dhoondhne wale jaga ko thik se chhaapna chahiye. Agar disease badhta hai, to 10-15 din ke andar dobara karna pad sakta hai, lekin 2-3 baar tak hi karna chahiye. Fungicide group ko badalna chahiye taki resistance na ho.
seed_question: What are the control measures for downy mildew disease in Gobhi Sarson (Indian mustard)?
seed_answer: Downy mildew is a serious fungal disease affecting Gobhi Sarson (Brassica napus) caused by the pathogen Hyaloperonospora parasitica (formerly Peronospora parasitica). This disease can significantly reduce crop yield and quality if not managed properly, particularly under cool and humid weather conditions prevalent in Punjab during the mustard growing season.   Disease Description and Symptoms: - Small light green lesions appear on the lower side of the leaves, which later enlarge, become more greyish, dry up, shrivel and tear easily.  - All aerial parts of the plants are attacked. The disease is severe on foliage and seed bearing parts.  - Greater deformity occurs in the stem.  - Relative humidity of more than 90% with temperature in the range of 10-20°C are conducive for disease development and spread.  - To avoid incidence of this disease, destroy diseased debris from the previous crop.  Integrated Disease Management: Cultural Practices:  • Use certified seeds of resistant/tolerant variety. • Follow timely sowing of crop. • Adopt proper field sanitation. • Follow proper crop rotation. • Destruct crop debris particularly stag heads of previous year crop. • Avoid over irrigation or water stagnation. • Apply potash in recommended dose   Botanical Management:  Treat the seeds with freshly prepared garlic bulb extract @ 2% (w/v).  Chemical Management: Fungicide application is essential when disease symptoms first appear or under favorable environmental conditions. Apply  • Metalaxyl 35% WS @ 0.75- 1.0 Kg/100 Kg seed • Metalaxyl M 4% + mancozeb 64% WP @ 1000 g in 400 l of water/acre • Metalaxyl 8% + mancozeb 64% WP @1000 g in 400 l of water/acre (white rust)   Ensure thorough coverage of both leaf surfaces during spraying. Repeat applications at 10-15 day intervals if disease persists, but do not exceed 2-3 sprays per season. Alternate between different fungicide groups to prevent resistance development.    Safety Precautions: When handling and applying fungicides, farmers must wear protective clothing including long-sleeved shirts, long pants, chemical-resistant gloves, protective goggles, and face masks to avoid skin contact and inhalation of spray mist. Mix chemicals in open, well-ventilated areas away from food and water sources. Do not eat, drink, or smoke during application. After spraying, wash hands, face, and exposed body parts thoroughly with soap and water. Wash contaminated clothing separately before reuse. Observe the Pre-Harvest Interval (PHI).   For further guidance or clarification, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

generated_question: What should I do if my mustard crop is getting downy mildew?
generated_answer: If your mustard crop has downy mildew, start by using certified seeds of resistant varieties. Apply potash as recommended and treat seeds with garlic extract. If the disease appears, spray Metalaxyl 35% WS or a mix of Metalaxyl and mancozeb. Make sure to cover both sides of the leaves. Spray every 10-15 days if needed, but no more than two or three times. Always wear protective gear when applying chemicals and follow safety steps.

seed_question: Where can I obtain spare parts for paddy seed drum equipment?
seed_answer: Paddy seed drum equipment is an essential farm machinery component used for mechanical seeding operations in rice cultivation. This equipment facilitates uniform and efficient seed placement, which is crucial for achieving optimal plant population and higher yields.   The paddy seed drum is a mechanical device used in seed drills or drum seeders for direct seeding of paddy. It ensures proper seed metering, uniform distribution, and appropriate depth placement. Regular maintenance and availability of spare parts are essential for uninterrupted farm operations during critical sowing periods.   Spare Parts Availability and Sources  For farmers in Tamil Nadu, particularly in Vellore district, spare parts for paddy seed drums can be obtained through multiple channels:    1. Agricultural Machinery Research Centre (AMRC): The primary contact for spare parts in your region is AMRC. Contact number: 044-29515322. They provide genuine spare parts and technical guidance for various agricultural machinery including seed drums.    2. State Agricultural Departments: Tamil Nadu Department of Agriculture maintains service centers and empanelled dealers who stock spare parts for commonly used farm equipment. Can contact TNAU for assistance as well contact no is 0422 661 1200    3. Krishi Vigyan Kendras (KVKs): Your nearest KVK can guide you to authorized dealers and service providers for farm machinery spare parts.    4. Manufacturer Authorized Dealers: Contact the original manufacturer's authorized service centers or dealers who maintain inventory of genuine spare parts.   Common Spare Parts Required Typical spare parts for paddy seed drums include:  - Seed metering wheels or drums  - Drive chains and sprockets  - Bearings and bushings  - Seed tubes and boots  - Furrow openers  - Covering devices  - Adjustment mechanisms   Always purchase genuine spare parts from authorized sources to ensure quality and compatibility. Maintain proper records of maintenance activities and spare parts replacement. Consider signing annual maintenance contracts with authorized service providers for timely support.   For further guidance or clarification, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.
